An agent can implement the criterion but cannot verify it is satisfied ('measurably helps' requires out-of-session evaluation against real revision outcomes).

3. **`REVIEW_RESULT` offline-analysis-readiness (AC item 8 / Success Criterion 5):** 'sufficient to reconstruct per-criterion FP/remediation analysis offline' — there is no schema, field list, or example event defined against which an agent can verify completeness. What constitutes 'sufficient' is opaque to in-session verification.

4. **CI-check pattern / external CI process (AC item last / Success Criterion 6):** 'checkable by an external CI process' and 'Docs: … the CI-check pattern' — while docs can be written in-session, verifying that an external CI process can actually consume the attestation requires a live integration that is out-of-session by definition. The docs AC is fine; the 'checkable by an external CI' assertion is not independently testable in-session.

None of these individually make the ticket uncomplete-able, but together they leave a material portion of the ACs un-verifiable in-session without additional specification.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. For experiment-grounded defaults: either inline the relevant result table (e.g., 'default chunk size = N criteria/call, budget = X tokens, depth thresholds = [A, B]') or restate the AC as 'defaults are documented and reference the session log location; values are placeholders pending experiment ticket completion' and file a follow-on.
2. For 'measurably help': replace with a proxy in-session check, e.g., 'each advisory finding includes a criterion reference, a specific location quote, a severity label, and a concrete suggested edit — format verified by a unit test or schema assertion'.
3. For `REVIEW_RESULT` sufficiency: add a minimum required field list (ticket_id, review_run_id, criterion_id, verdict, severity, finding_fingerprint, timestamp, model_used, override_flag) so an agent can write a schema-validation test.
4. For the CI-check AC: split into (a) 'attestation format documented and parseable by a provided reference script' (in-session verifiable) and (b) 'live CI integration' (follow-on F11).

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)': solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard; 'Criteria registry: declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)'; 'Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing; escalation by size+complexity+centrality; per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing'
findings.finding:
Three distinct potential over-engineering / NIH concerns are present, but all require codebase access to confirm:

1. **Criteria registry + generic orchestrator**: The plan says "reuse rebar.llm extension points" — if that orchestrator already exists and is genuinely reused, no concern. If a new generic orchestrator is being built on top of an existing one solely for this gate (one call-site at epic launch), that would be a Rule-of-Three violation. Cannot determine from plan text alone.

2. **Toolchain assembly (solidlsp + ast-grep + syft + scc + lizard)**: Each tool is individually justified (polyglot, fail-open), but the combination of five distinct external tools for a single structural floor is potentially over-assembled. It is unclear whether any of these are already integrated in rebar ("solidlsp already in repo" is noted for solidlsp only). The plan does not clarify which of the remaining four are already present vs. net-new integrations.

3. **Configuration surface proliferation**: The plan introduces multiple independent knobs — chunking granularity (project knob), budget cap (per-plan), depth posture, per-criterion advisory/blocking, INDETERMINATE blocking policy, escalation model — each with 'sensible defaults'. The Rule-of-Three concern is whether projects will realistically exercise all these axes before defaults are validated empirically (AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'). The experiment results are referenced but not yet in-hand, meaning several knobs may be premature configuration surface until experiments complete. This is a real over-engineering risk if defaults end up covering 95%+ of cases.

None of these individually appear to be clear-cut NIH (no obvious existing library is being reinvented). The concerns are scope/complexity-creep rather than pure NIH.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. Explicitly state which of the five tools (ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard) are already repo-dependencies vs. net-new, so reviewers can assess integration cost and NIH risk.
2. Consider deferring the most speculative configuration knobs (e.g., per-criterion blocking policy, escalation model) to follow-on work until experiment baselines are available — they are already partially covered by follow-on items F2–F4.
3. Add a one-line note confirming the criteria orchestrator reuses an existing rebar.llm runner without a new abstraction layer, or call out that a new layer is required and justify it.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all 12 bullet points) and 'Follow-on work' section
findings.finding: This ticket is scoped as an epic but has NO child tickets defined within the plan itself — only a 'Follow-on work' list of F1–F12 items explicitly deferred. The epic's Acceptance Criteria (12 bullets) encompass at minimum 8–10 distinct, non-trivial engineering deliverables that span multiple subsystems: (1) Layer-1 deterministic criteria with polyglot tooling (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc/lizard), (2) Layer-2 advisory LLM criteria (~12 individual criteria), (3) a criteria registry + generic orchestrator, (4) attestation signing + material-event-commit binding, (5) `claim` integration with force-override, (6) configurable chunking + depth routing + centrality/blast-radius computation + budget cap with checkpointing, (7) feedback-authoring coaching spec enforcement, (8) `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event + reducer skip, (9) per-criterion config system, (10) docs. Each of these is independently complex enough to be a multi-session unit of work. The plan acknowledges this implicitly ('Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b') but does not decompose the epic into child tickets. The design summary says 'Routing pivots on has_children + complexity' — yet this parent ticket itself has no children, violating the very pattern the feature is meant to enforce. One-shotting this as a single unit of work is not coherent; the plan provides no decomposition into children.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose this epic into a set of linked child tickets, each executable in one focused session. A suggested vertical-slice decomposition (not horizontal layers): (C1) Layer-1 deterministic floor — readiness-shape + AC/DD quality checks (pure structural, no tooling); (C2) Layer-1 tooling integration — file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep), package existence (syft+registry), oversize (scc/lizard), fail-open harness; (C3) Criteria registry + generic orchestrator (declarative descriptors, rebar.llm reuse, chunking config, budget cap, checkpointing, INDETERMINATE outcome); (C4) Attestation + material-event-commit binding + invalidation + `claim` integration + force-override; (C5) Layer-2 advisory criteria — subset 1 (measurability, user/problem, coherence, ambiguity, intent-fidelity); (C6) Layer-2 advisory criteria — subset 2 (testing-plan, verification/termination, edit-set/scope, child-coverage, assumption/premise, anti-slop, broad pass); (C7) Depth routing + centrality/blast-radius computation + escalation logic; (C8) `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event + reducer skip + override logging; (C9) Per-criterion config (advisory/blocking-with-override); (C10) Docs. Each child should carry its own AC and be the unit on which the new gate fires. Also add the decomposition as explicit children in rebar so the gate's own has_children routing applies to this epic.

findings.location: Success Criteria and Acceptance Criteria sections — no dedicated testing-plan subsection present
findings.finding: The plan describes what to build in detail but contains no explicit testing plan for the gate itself. There is no mention of: (1) how Layer-1 deterministic checks will be tested (e.g., unit tests per criterion, synthetic tickets with known structural defects, polyglot fixture projects); (2) how the fail-open behavior is verified (simulated LSP/tool unavailability); (3) how Layer-2 advisory criteria are exercised (golden-set inputs with expected verdicts/findings); (4) how the `claim` integration's fast attestation check is tested (valid cert, stale cert, missing cert, --force override paths); (5) how the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event schema and reducer-skip behavior are contractually covered; (6) RED-before-GREEN ordering or any mention of TDD posture. The AC item 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' gestures at empirical grounding but is about configuration, not test coverage. The linked session log (63cc-ec94-4792-464b) may contain a testing strategy, but it is not surfaced in the self-contained epic summary, and the plan explicitly claims to be self-contained.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Testing Strategy' subsection (or a dedicated AC bullet) that at minimum covers: (a) unit/integration tests for each Layer-1 criterion with pass/fail/skip fixture inputs; (b) a synthetic polyglot fixture suite that exercises fail-open paths (unsupported lang, tool crash, timeout); (c) a golden-set harness for Layer-2 advisory criteria covering at least one PASS, one AMBIGUOUS, and one FAIL case per criterion; (d) contract tests for the REVIEW_RESULT event schema and the reducer-skip invariant; (e) end-to-end claim-gate tests covering all four attestation states (present+valid, present+stale, absent, --force override); (f) explicit statement of RED-before-GREEN or equivalent test-first posture where applicable.

findings.location: Context section, paragraph 1: 'nothing checks that the ticket's implementation plan is sound before the agent executes it. Flawed plans ... get executed anyway, and early errors compound over autonomous agent trajectories (~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects).'
Design summary, multiple sections
findings.finding:
The plan makes several assertions about the existing codebase and environment that an executor would need to verify before relying on them:

1. **'solidlsp (already in repo)'** — The plan states solidlsp is already present in the repository as if it is a confirmed, ready-to-use integration point. An executor would need to verify this is true, that it is at a usable version, and that it actually exposes the LSP interface the gate needs (e.g., file/symbol/import resolution).

2. **'reusing rebar.llm extension points' / 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'** — The plan asserts that rebar.llm already has a generic orchestrator with catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points compatible with the criteria-registry pattern described. An executor must confirm these extension points exist, are stable, and are sufficient to plug in new criteria without significant rework.

3. **'extends the close-gate signing'** — The attestation design assumes the close-gate already has a signing mechanism that is directly extensible. An executor must verify the close-gate signing API is factored for reuse, not tightly coupled to close-specific logic.

4. **'reducer skips the type'** — The plan asserts the reducer can be made to ignore the new REVIEW_RESULT event type. An executor must confirm the reducer architecture allows selective event-type skipping (e.g., a whitelist/blacklist pattern exists) without requiring a refactor.

5. **'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time'** — Implies infrastructure for computing centrality already exists or is trivially available at plan time. This would need verification.

6. **'ast-grep (resolution/callers)'** — Asserts ast-grep is available and usable for caller/resolution queries in the repo's polyglot context. Needs verification it is installed/integrated and supports the required query patterns.

None of these can be confirmed or denied from the plan text alone — they require inspection of the live codebase.
findings.suggested_edit:
Add a short 'Verified preconditions' subsection (or annotate each assumption inline) that an executor must check before beginning implementation:
- Confirm solidlsp is present, at the expected version, and exposes file/symbol/import resolution.
- Confirm rebar.llm exposes catalog/contracts/prompt/runner as stable extension points.
- Confirm close-gate signing is factored for reuse (not close-specific).
- Confirm reducer supports selective event-type skipping.
- Confirm ast-grep is installed and supports the required query patterns.
- Confirm or scope the centrality/blast-radius computation infrastructure.
If any assumption is unconfirmed, flag it as a prerequisite task before proceeding with the dependent AC.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment results)'
findings.finding:
Most acceptance criteria map clearly to described design steps, but one AC item — 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment results)' — references external experiment work that is neither described in the plan nor explicitly called out as a prerequisite deliverable. It is unclear whether this experiment work is (a) already done and the results are available, (b) in-flight in a parallel ticket, or (c) expected to be performed as part of this epic. If (c), there is a gap between the described steps and the stated end-state: no step in the plan describes running, evaluating, or consuming those experiments, so an agent cannot know when this AC is satisfied or how to stop.

A secondary, smaller concern: the plan describes Layer-2 criteria as 'advisory by default' and the verdict model as 'INDETERMINATE = non-blocking by default', but the success criterion 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings that measurably help an agent revise the plan' introduces a measurability requirement with no described measurement mechanism or threshold. There is no step in the plan that produces the evidence needed to check this SC, making it hard to know when the work is truly done.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. For the experiments AC: add an explicit statement clarifying the dependency — either 'Experiment results are a prerequisite (ticket X); this epic consumes them' or, if they must be produced here, add a concrete step (e.g., 'Run N-sample ablation comparing chunking strategies; record results; set defaults from best-performing config') so the agent knows what 'done' looks like.
2. For the measurability SC: either reframe it as a structural/qualitative bar ('findings include grounded criterion ref, file/section location, severity label, and suggested edit') that an agent can verify deterministically, or move the 'measurably help' claim to follow-on work (F10 calibrated abstention / labeled gold set seems the right home).

findings.location: ## Follow-on work section and ## Acceptance Criteria (all 12 ACs listed as a single unit)
findings.finding: This ticket is an epic that lists 12 distinct acceptance criteria spanning at minimum 8–10 substantially different implementation concerns: (1) Layer-1 deterministic criteria with polyglot tooling (LSP, ast-grep, syft, scc/lizard), (2) Layer-2 advisory criteria (11+ named sub-criteria), (3) a criteria registry + generic orchestrator, (4) attestation signing + invalidation logic, (5) `claim` integration, (6) configurable chunking + depth routing + escalation + budget cap + checkpointing + INDETERMINATE outcome, (7) coaching/feedback-authoring conformance, (8) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip + override logging, (9) per-criterion config knobs, (10) experiment-grounded defaults, (11) docs, and (12) bug exemption routing. The plan itself defers 12 named follow-on items (F1–F12) and explicitly references a separate session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) as the plan-of-record. No child tickets are present in the plan text; the decomposition is only gestured at via the follow-on list, which covers *post*-epic work rather than the sub-tasks needed to execute *this* epic. An agent attempting to execute this as a single leaf ticket would face an unbounded scope: multiple new subsystems (two-layer review engine, polyglot LSP/ast-grep/syft integration, signing infrastructure, sidecar event type, config surface, orchestrator, docs) that collectively span thousands of lines of novel code across many modules. This is precisely the 'too big to one-shot' failure mode the ticket itself warns about in its Context section ('~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'). The plan acknowledges it is an epic but provides no decomposition into child tickets, leaving the execution surface entirely undefined.
findings.suggested_edit:
Decompose this epic into at least the following child tickets before claiming it, with this parent ticket serving only as the coordination/tracking shell:
1. **Layer-1 deterministic checks** (readiness shape, file/symbol resolution, package existence, oversize, task-DAG validity, AC/DD quality, destructive sniff) — one ticket.
2. **Layer-2 advisory criteria** (all 11+ named sub-criteria + triggered overlays + bounded broad pass) — one ticket (or further split by criteria group if large).
3. **Criteria registry + generic orchestrator** (declarative descriptors, reuse rebar.llm extension points) — one ticket.
4. **Attestation subsystem** (signing, material-event-commit binding, invalidation, coverage record) — one ticket.
5. **`claim` integration** (fast attestation check, --force+justification override) — one ticket.
6. **Execution engine** (chunking config, depth routing, model escalation, budget cap, chunk-atomic checkpointing, INDETERMINATE outcome) — one ticket.
7. **REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event** (event schema, reducer skip, override logging, fingerprints) — one ticket.
8. **Config surface + experiment-grounded defaults** — one ticket.
9. **Docs** — one ticket.
Each child ticket should be independently executable in one agent session and reference this epic as its parent.

findings.location: Design summary – 'Polyglot tooling' bullet; Acceptance Criteria – Layer-1 and Layer-2 AC bullets; Follow-on list
findings.finding: The plan names high-level system concepts (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard, rebar.llm, rebar's completion-verifier, the claim command, the REVIEW_RESULT reducer) but does not enumerate the specific files, modules, classes, or symbols that will be created or modified. There is no explicit list of: (a) which existing rebar source files/modules will be touched to wire the gate into the open→in_progress transition, (b) what new modules/files the implementation introduces (e.g. criteria registry, orchestrator, attestation signer, sidecar emitter, routing logic), or (c) which existing consumer call-sites of the claim path, the signing subsystem, or the reducer need to be updated. Because this is a high-level epic plan rather than a story-level implementation ticket, that level of detail may be intentionally deferred — but reviewers or implementing agents cannot verify edit-set accuracy or blast radius from this text alone. Specifically ambiguous: whether the completion-verifier's signing code is extended in-place vs. forked (AC: 'extends the close-gate signing'), which reducer event-handling code must be updated to ignore REVIEW_RESULT, and whether any CI integration helpers are in-scope now or only in follow-on F11.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / modules in scope' subsection that lists at minimum: (1) the existing module(s) that implement the claim/open→in_progress transition and will receive the attestation-check hook; (2) the existing reducer that must learn to skip REVIEW_RESULT events; (3) the existing signing/attestation module to be extended; (4) the new modules to be created (criteria registry, orchestrator, Layer-1 checks, Layer-2 checks, routing, sidecar emitter). Also explicitly state whether F11 (CI integration helpers) is out of scope for this epic, so the blast radius of the signing change is bounded.

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: The plan defers the concrete default values for chunking, budget caps, and depth to experiment results that are only referenced externally ('see linked experiment work' / 'session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b'). An executing agent cannot determine these numeric defaults without access to that session log or the experiment outputs. If those artifacts are not directly accessible to the agent, this is an undercooked placeholder that forces a stopping question or an arbitrary guess.
findings.suggested_edit: Inline the empirically derived defaults (e.g., criteria-per-LLM-call chunk size, per-plan token/cost budget cap, depth tiers per complexity score) directly in the plan or in a linked, agent-readable document. Even approximate ranges ('chunk size: 3–5 criteria; budget cap: ~$0.50/plan; depth: shallow=1 pass / deep=2 passes + escalation') would make the AC self-contained.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Criteria registry: each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)' and 'Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing … escalation by size+complexity+centrality … per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing and an INDETERMINATE outcome' and 'Per-criterion config: advisory (default) | blocking-with-override'
findings.finding:
Three areas warrant scrutiny under the anti-slop / over-engineering criterion, but all require codebase access to reach a firm verdict:

1. **Criteria registry as a generic orchestrator (potential NIH / gratuitous abstraction).** The plan notes it should "reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner," which is the right instinct. However, introducing a *new* declarative-descriptor registry + generic orchestrator on top of an existing runner is a Rule-of-Three risk if the existing completion-verifier already has an orchestration layer that could be extended. Without seeing what rebar.llm's existing catalog/runner already provides, it is impossible to say whether this is a clean extension or a redundant layer used only at this one call-site.

2. **Configuration surface proliferation.** The plan introduces at least six independent config knobs: (a) chunking granularity, (b) budget cap, (c) depth posture, (d) per-criterion blocking flag, (e) INDETERMINATE blocking behavior, (f) escalation model selection (by size+complexity+centrality). Each knob is justified individually, but the cumulative surface is large. If the completion-verifier already has a config subsystem, adding a parallel one for the plan-review gate could be duplication; if it extends the same subsystem, the risk is lower.

3. **Centrality / blast-radius computation at plan time.** This is called out as a trigger for model escalation. Depending on what dependency/graph tooling already exists in rebar, this may be a net-new computation layer or a thin wrapper. The plan does not reference any existing rebar graph/centrality utility.

None of these is definitively over-engineered from plan text alone — the design explicitly calls for reuse of rebar.llm extension points and the fail-open polyglot tooling (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard) appears to leverage already-present-in-repo tools. The concern is whether the *orchestration and config* layers are genuinely new abstractions justified by the problem or duplications of existing rebar infrastructure.
findings.suggested_edit:
Before finalizing, audit the following in the live codebase and add a short note to the plan addressing each:
- Does rebar.llm's existing catalog/runner cover the orchestration need directly, or does a new registry layer add real value? If the former, remove the separate registry and extend in place.
- Does the completion-verifier already expose a config schema that can be extended for this gate's knobs, rather than introducing a parallel config surface?
- Is there an existing graph/centrality utility in rebar that the escalation trigger can call, rather than computing blast-radius from scratch?
If any of these confirm duplication, collapse the layers and update the ACs accordingly.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)' section; 'Execution' section; 'Attestation' section; AC bullets referencing rebar.llm and close-gate signing
findings.finding:
The plan makes several assertions about the existing codebase that an executor would need to verify before relying on them:

1. **"solidlsp (already in repo)"** — The plan treats solidlsp as a known-present dependency. An executor needs to confirm it is actually present, at what version, and with what interface contract, before building the Layer-1 LSP-based file/symbol resolution on top of it.

2. **"reusing rebar.llm extension points" / "reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner"** — The plan asserts that rebar.llm exposes suitable catalog, contract, prompt, and runner extension points that can be reused without modification. The executor needs to verify that these extension points exist, are stable/public, and are capable of hosting a generic orchestrator for the criteria registry.

3. **"extends the close-gate signing"** — The plan assumes the close-gate signing mechanism already exists in a form that can be extended (same key format, same binding model, compatible invalidation logic). The executor needs to confirm the current close-gate signing implementation is actually extensible rather than monolithic or tightly coupled to the close context.

4. **"ast-grep (resolution/callers)"** — Similar to solidlsp, it is stated as an available tool without confirming it is installed/vendored in the repo or available in the runtime environment.

5. **"syft + registry + LLM-fallback (deps)"** and **"scc + lizard (size)"** — These tools are listed as the tooling layer for package existence and size checks, but the plan does not confirm whether they are already integrated, merely available in the environment, or need to be newly added as dependencies.

6. **"a reducer-IGNORED SIDECAR `REVIEW_RESULT` event"** — The plan asserts the reducer already has an ignore/skip mechanism for unknown event types, or that one can be trivially added. The executor needs to verify the reducer's current handling of unknown event types before treating 'reducer skips the type' as a given.

7. **"Full design detail lives in session log `63cc-ec94-4792-464b`"** — The plan delegates a large portion of its design rationale to an external session log. If that log is not reliably accessible to the executor, critical design decisions (criteria list, budget defaults, experiment results referenced in AC bullet 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results') are unverified assumptions embedded in the plan.
findings.suggested_edit:
Add a short 'Verified prerequisites' sub-section (or an explicit checklist item under AC) that confirms each of the following before execution begins:
- solidlsp presence, version, and interface contract in the repo.
- ast-grep, syft, scc, and lizard availability/installation path.
- rebar.llm extension points (catalog/contracts/prompt/runner) are stable public APIs suitable for generic orchestration.
- The close-gate signing implementation is extensible in the way described.
- The event reducer already handles or can be cheaply patched to ignore unknown event types.
- The session log `63cc-ec94-4792-464b` is accessible and its experiment results are available to inform the chunking/budget defaults AC bullet.

Alternatively, flag each of these with an explicit '(TO VERIFY)' annotation inline so an executor knows which assumptions require a codebase-read step before proceeding.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC item: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).'
findings.finding:
Several acceptance criteria depend on artifacts or outcomes that exist outside the plan itself, making in-session completability ambiguous:

1. **Experiment-grounded defaults (AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment results)')** — The criterion requires empirical experiment results to already exist and be accessible to the implementing agent. If those experiments are a follow-on activity or are only referenced in an external session log ('session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b'), an agent working this ticket cannot independently verify or produce those defaults within the session.

2. **AC: 'Criteria registry … reusing rebar.llm extension points'** — Verifying this requires inspecting the existing rebar.llm codebase for the specific extension-point contracts. Without codebase access the agent would need to assume they exist and are suitable; if they don't or are incompatible, the criterion is not completable as stated.

3. **AC: 'Layer-2 advisory criteria … measurably help an agent revise the plan' (Success Criterion #3)** — 'Measurably help' implies empirical measurement across multiple agent runs. This cannot be verified within a single working session by the implementing agent; it reads as a post-deployment/operational outcome, not a build-time check.

4. **AC: 'Docs: how the gate works … and the CI-check pattern'** — Documentation is completable in-session, but 'CI-check pattern' implies an external CI system that can consume the attestation. Verifying the CI integration path requires a live or simulated CI environment that may not be available in-session.

The bulk of the structural ACs (Layer-1 implementation, Layer-2 criteria, attestation signing, claim integration, REVIEW_RESULT event, per-criterion config, fail-open tooling) are concrete and in-session completable. The ambiguity is isolated to the four areas above.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. For the experiment-grounded defaults AC, add: 'The linked experiment results are pre-computed and committed to the repo before this ticket is claimed; the implementing agent reads them from [specific path].' If they are not yet available, split this into a prerequisite ticket.
2. For 'measurably help an agent revise the plan', replace with a proxy that is in-session verifiable, e.g.: 'Layer-2 findings conform to the coaching spec (grounded, specific, severity-ranked, checklist-form) as verified by a rubric test fixture with ≥N synthetic plan examples.'
3. For the CI-check pattern, scope the AC to 'the attestation is in a documented, machine-readable format checkable by a CI script; a reference script is included in the repo' — removing any dependency on a live external CI system.
4. For rebar.llm extension-point reuse, add a pointer to the specific interface/contract file so the agent can verify compatibility without full codebase exploration.

findings.location: Design summary ('Execution' bullet) vs. AC bullet 11 ('Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)')
findings.finding:
The plan body says defaults are set 'EMPIRICALLY via experiments' and the AC requires those defaults to be 'grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work),' but no experiment work is described in the plan or listed as an in-scope deliverable. The linked session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) is the only reference, and it is external and unquoted. This leaves the AC partially orphaned: there is no in-plan description of what the experiments are, who runs them, or when results are available, so an agent executing the epic cannot know when this AC is satisfied. This is a coverage gap between the AC and the plan body.

Additionally, the 'Configurable chunking … escalation by size+complexity+centrality (centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time)' AC bullet is partially described in the Design summary ('Execution' bullet) but the term 'blast-radius' appears only in the Design summary and not in the Success Criteria or AC preamble text, creating a minor terminology asymmetry (the AC uses 'centrality/blast-radius' as a compound; the Design summary uses 'centrality' and 'blast-radius' separately).

Finally, the 'intent fidelity (2-step blind-restate)' criterion in AC Layer-2 bullet is not described at all in the Design summary — there is no mention of a blind-restate step or its mechanics anywhere in the plan body, leaving this criterion body-orphaned.

No outright duplicate requirements were found across the AC bullets.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. Add a short paragraph or sub-bullet in the Design summary (or a new 'Experiments' section) describing what experiments are needed, what they measure, and how results feed into the shipped defaults — so AC bullet 11 has a corresponding plan-body description an agent can execute against.
2. Add a brief description of the 'intent fidelity (2-step blind-restate)' mechanic to the Layer-2 portion of the Design summary (e.g., 'Intent fidelity: reviewer re-states the plan's goal blindly from the criteria alone, then diffs against the description to surface hidden assumption gaps').
3. Normalize 'centrality/blast-radius' to one canonical compound term used consistently in both the Design summary and the AC.

findings.location: ## Context, paragraphs 1–2
Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria sections — no dedicated testing subsection exists
findings.finding: The plan describes the system being built in detail (criteria registry, orchestrator, attestation, sidecar events, routing, etc.) but contains no explicit testing plan for how the gate itself will be verified before delivery. There is no mention of: (1) unit tests for individual Layer-1 deterministic checks (e.g., file-resolution, oversize signals, destructive-sniff); (2) unit/integration tests for Layer-2 advisory criteria and the feedback-authoring pipeline; (3) RED-before-GREEN ordering or TDD intent; (4) how fail-open behavior is tested (i.e., that unsupported language/crashed tool yields PASS, not FAIL); (5) boundary/failure-path coverage for the attestation signing and invalidation logic; (6) how the `claim` fast-check integration is tested end-to-end; (7) how the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event shape is validated. The AC item 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' implies empirical experiments drive defaults, but the experiment protocol and how results translate into acceptance evidence is not described. It is possible testing conventions are captured in the referenced session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) or in an implicit rebar project-wide test standard — this cannot be confirmed without codebase/session access — hence AMBIGUOUS rather than FAIL.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Testing approach' subsection (or expand the Acceptance Criteria) covering at minimum: (a) unit tests for each Layer-1 check including fail-open paths (skipped tool → PASS); (b) integration/behavioral tests for Layer-2 feedback authoring (grounded finding format, severity cap, checklist shape); (c) end-to-end test for the open→in_progress gate (valid cert passes, missing/stale cert blocks, --force override logs correctly); (d) attestation invalidation tests (material edit voids sig, non-material edit does not); (e) sidecar event schema validation test; (f) experiment acceptance criteria (what numeric result on which metric constitutes 'chunking/budget defaults grounded'). RED-before-GREEN ordering should be stated explicitly if TDD is expected.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet 11: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).'
findings.finding:
Most of the plan's acceptance criteria are well-formed and collectively describe a reachable end-state, but two gaps introduce ambiguity about whether the described work actually closes the epic:

1. **Experiment dependency is unresolved in-plan.** AC item 11 requires defaults to be 'grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work),' but no experiment ticket/work is defined, scoped, or referenced concretely within this epic. If the experiments are not yet done (or not yet filed), the agent cannot satisfy this criterion from the described steps alone. The gap between 'what we'll do' and 'what done looks like' is real: the plan never says who runs the experiments, when, or what the gating relationship is.

2. **Layer-1 'oversize signals' termination is implicit.** The design mentions 'scc + lizard (size)' but does not specify the concrete threshold or signal definition that would let a reviewer (or automated check) declare the oversize criterion satisfied. Without a measurable threshold, an agent cannot determine when it has finished implementing this check correctly.

3. **'Docs' AC is underspecified for done-ness.** The final AC bullet ('Docs: how the gate works…') lists five topics but gives no structural requirement (e.g., a specific file, section headings, or review sign-off). An agent can mark this done at almost any fidelity level, making termination ambiguous.

None of these individually block execution, but together they mean the agent may reach a state that passes all mechanically-checkable ACs while the experiment-grounded defaults and documentation remain incomplete.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. For the experiment dependency: either (a) add an explicit prerequisite ticket reference and state 'this AC is blocked until experiment ticket #X is closed' or (b) replace 'grounded in experiment results' with an interim placeholder value (e.g., 'defaults ship as [chunk=1, budget=N tokens, depth=standard] pending experiment calibration in follow-on F-exp') so the AC is satisfiable now.
2. For oversize signals: specify the concrete threshold definition, e.g., 'file > 500 LOC (scc) or cyclomatic complexity > 20 (lizard) emits an oversize PASS/FAIL signal' — even if the values are later tuned.
3. For Docs: add a minimum structure requirement, e.g., 'a `docs/plan-review-gate.md` covering the six listed topics exists and is linked from the repo README.'

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' and Design: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly defers the concrete values for three operationally critical knobs — chunking granularity, per-plan budget cap, and depth thresholds — to experiment results that are referenced but not reproduced in the plan ('see linked experiment work', session log '63cc-ec94-4792-464b'). An executing agent cannot act on 'set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' without either having access to those experiment results or performing the experiments themselves. If the session log is inaccessible or the experiments are incomplete, the agent will be forced to either pick arbitrary defaults (wrong) or stop and ask (violation of the criterion). The plan gives no fallback range or seed values to use while experiments are pending.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) inline the specific numeric defaults (e.g., 'default chunk size = N criteria per call; budget cap = $X or T tokens; depth = shallow for complexity<3 / deep for complexity≥3') derived from the experiments, or (b) if experiments are not yet complete, add a 'pending experiment' placeholder with an explicit interim default the agent may use (e.g., 'until experiments complete, ship with chunk=3, budget_cap=0.50 USD, depth=full') so the executor has an unambiguous starting point and need not pause.

findings.location: Design summary — multiple sub-sections
findings.finding:
The plan makes several specific assertions about existing codebase infrastructure that an executor would need to verify before relying on them:

1. **'solidlsp (already in repo)'** — The plan states solidlsp is already present and implies it is ready to serve as the LSP backend for file/symbol resolution. An executor needs to confirm solidlsp is actually importable/runnable in this repo, what version it is, and whether its API matches what Layer-1 expects.

2. **'reusing rebar.llm extension points'** — The criteria registry and orchestrator are described as reusing `rebar.llm`'s catalog/contracts/prompt/runner. An executor needs to verify that these extension points exist, are stable/public interfaces, and support the declarative-descriptor pattern described.

3. **'extends the close-gate signing'** — Attestation design says the plan-review signing 'extends the completion-verifier / close-gate signing.' An executor needs to confirm the close-gate signing mechanism is abstracted into a reusable component (vs. inlined in the close flow), and that its API accepts the additional coverage/material-event fields this plan requires.

4. **'reducer-IGNORED SIDECAR REVIEW_RESULT event … reducer skips the type'** — The plan asserts the event reducer already has (or will trivially add) a skip/passthrough path for unknown event types. An executor needs to verify whether the reducer's current dispatch logic silently ignores unknown types or raises an error, and whether adding a new type truly requires no reducer change.

5. **'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time'** — Implies tooling or a module for computing centrality already exists or is straightforward to derive. An executor needs to confirm whether a graph/centrality utility is available in the repo or whether it must be built from scratch.

None of these can be confirmed from the plan text alone; all require inspection of the live codebase.
findings.suggested_edit:
Add a short 'Prerequisite verifications' section (or inline checklist in the Design summary) that explicitly lists each assumed capability and directs the executor to validate it before starting implementation. For example:
- Confirm solidlsp importable and API surface; document the method signatures Layer-1 will call.
- Confirm rebar.llm catalog/contracts/prompt/runner are public extension points; link to their interfaces.
- Confirm close-gate signing is extracted into a shared module; if not, extract it as the first sub-task.
- Confirm reducer dispatch behavior on unknown event types (silent skip vs. error); add a regression test.
- Confirm or build centrality/blast-radius utility; scope as an explicit sub-task if absent.

findings.location: Design summary (Polyglot tooling, Execution, Attestation sections) and Acceptance Criteria bullets (Layer-1, Layer-2, Attestation, claim integration, REVIEW_RESULT)
findings.finding: The plan describes the feature in architectural/conceptual terms but does not name any specific files, modules, classes, or symbols that will be created or modified in the rebar codebase. For example: (1) which existing module houses the `claim` command that will need the fast attestation check wired in? (2) where does the completion-verifier live, and will its signing/attestation logic be extended or duplicated? (3) which existing rebar.llm extension points (catalog/contracts/prompt/runner) are the intended reuse targets — their file paths are not cited. (4) solidlsp is noted as 'already in repo' but no path is given. (5) the `REVIEW_RESULT` event requires the reducer to be updated to skip the new type — which reducer file(s) are affected? Without the live codebase it is impossible to confirm whether these targets exist at the named paths, whether callers of `claim` or the reducer need updating across the codebase, or whether the blast radius of wiring in the new gate is fully scoped. The plan acknowledges the design lives in a session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) not included here, so key implementation targets may be there but are invisible to this review.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / symbols touched' subsection (even if approximate) that lists: the `claim` command entry-point file, the completion-verifier module being extended, the reducer file(s) to be updated for REVIEW_RESULT skip, the rebar.llm orchestrator/runner files being reused, and the solidlsp integration path. Mark each as 'new' or 'modified'. This makes blast-radius visible and allows callers/consumers to be checked before execution begins.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC items 1, 6, 11, 12; Success Criteria SC-4 ('sensible defaults shipped'); SC-6 ('checkable by an external CI process')
findings.finding:
Most ACs are structurally measurable (implement X, emit Y, check Z), but several have verifiability gaps that make full single-session completability ambiguous:

1. **AC-11 ('Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results')** — the experiments are described as 'linked experiment work' but the experiment results themselves are not present in this plan. An agent cannot ground the defaults without access to those results; if the linked session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) is not machine-readable within the session, this criterion is uncompletable in-session.

2. **AC-12 ('Docs: how the gate works…')** — Documentation completeness is inherently hard to verify objectively. There is no stated acceptance bar (e.g., 'docs cover these N sections', 'passes a doc-lint check'). An agent can write docs, but 'complete and correct' is unmeasurable without a concrete checklist or structural template.

3. **SC-6 / AC-4 ('checkable by an external CI process')** — 'Checkable by an external CI process' implies a live or simulated CI integration test. It is unclear whether a real external CI endpoint is required or whether a local simulation suffices. If live CI is required, this cannot be validated in-session.

4. **SC-3 ('measurably help an agent revise the plan')** — 'Measurably help' is not operationalised (no metric, no threshold, no observable proxy within the session). This reads more like a design intention than a verifiable acceptance criterion for a single implementation session.

5. **AC-6 ('Defaults … grounded in experiment results') + escalation routing** — The criteria for centrality/blast-radius computation are mentioned but no concrete algorithm or output format is specified, making it hard to verify correctness without a reference implementation or test fixture.
findings.suggested_edit: For AC-11: add an inline table or reference appendix summarising the experiment results (or explicitly state 'if experiment data is unavailable, ship a documented placeholder default and mark the criterion deferred'). For AC-12: add a concrete documentation outline (e.g., 'must contain sections: Overview, Config reference, Fail-open behaviour, Cert semantics, CI-check example'). For SC-3: replace 'measurably help' with a proxy metric verifiable in-session, e.g., 'each advisory finding includes a grounded criterion reference, a specific file/section location, and a concrete suggested edit; a review of 3 synthetic test plans produces ≥1 finding per plan'. For SC-6/AC-4: clarify that CI checkability is verified via a local simulation or a documented CLI command, not a live external system.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all 12 bullet points) and Design Summary (all subsections)
findings.finding: This ticket is scoped as a single epic with 12 distinct, substantive acceptance criteria spanning at least 8–10 independently deliverable subsystems: (1) Layer-1 deterministic criteria implementation with polyglot tooling (LSP, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard), (2) Layer-2 advisory criteria implementation (~12 named advisory criteria + overlays + broad pass), (3) criteria registry + generic orchestrator, (4) attestation signing + material-event-commit binding + invalidation logic, (5) `claim` integration with force-override, (6) configurable chunking + depth routing + centrality/blast-radius computation + escalation + budget-capping + checkpointing + INDETERMINATE outcome, (7) feedback-authoring conformance to coaching spec, (8) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip + fingerprints + override logging, (9) per-criterion blocking config knob, (10) bug exemption routing, (11) experiment-grounded defaults (implying experiments must be run or results consumed), and (12) documentation. Each of these is a meaningful engineering body of work in its own right. The plan itself acknowledges this is an 'epic' and lists 12 follow-on tickets (F1–F12), but does NOT decompose the epic into child tickets — the 'Follow-on work' section explicitly defers that decomposition, leaving this as a single unit of claimed work with no children defined. The plan states 'Routing pivots on has_children + complexity' and 'Review depth scales with complexity + has_children', implying the system itself would flag this ticket as high-complexity with no children — a posture inconsistent with the gate being built. An autonomous agent tasked with executing this single ticket would need to deliver all 12 ACs coherently in one session, which is not feasible and is the exact failure mode the gate is designed to prevent.
findings.suggested_edit:
Decompose this epic into child tickets before execution. A recommended vertical-slice decomposition (each slice is independently mergeable and testable):
1. **Core infrastructure**: criteria registry + generic orchestrator + rebar.llm integration
2. **Attestation subsystem**: signing, material-event-commit binding, invalidation-on-material-edit, coverage record (extending close-gate signing)
3. **`claim` integration**: fast signed-attestation check + --force/justification override
4. **Layer-1 deterministic criteria**: all named checks (readiness shape, file/symbol resolution, package existence, oversize signals, DAG validity, AC/DD quality, destructive sniff) with polyglot fail-open tooling
5. **Layer-2 advisory criteria**: all 12+ named advisory criteria + triggered overlays + broad pass + coaching-spec-conformant feedback authoring
6. **Routing + execution engine**: chunking config, depth routing (complexity + has_children), centrality/blast-radius computation, escalation logic, budget capping, chunk-atomic checkpointing, INDETERMINATE outcome
7. **REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event**: reducer skip, fingerprint schema, override logging, offline-analysis-ready design
8. **Experiment runs + defaults**: empirical chunking/budget/depth defaults from experiments
9. **Documentation**: gate mechanics, config surface, fail-open behavior, cert semantics, CI-check pattern

The epic description is excellent as a parent context document; it should be retained as the parent ticket with these children linked beneath it.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points' and AC: 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points'
findings.finding:
Several design elements warrant an anti-slop/over-engineering check, but full assessment requires codebase access:

1. **Criteria registry + generic orchestrator**: The plan explicitly claims reuse of 'rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'. Whether this is a genuine reuse or a new abstraction layer that parallels an existing one cannot be determined without inspecting the repo. If the existing completion-verifier already has a similar dispatch mechanism, a new 'criteria registry + generic orchestrator' could be duplicating rather than reusing.

2. **Per-criterion blocking config knob**: The plan introduces a per-criterion `advisory | blocking-with-override` configuration dimension on top of the project-level `risk posture`, `chunking`, and `budget caps` — that is at least four independent configuration axes. This is a meaningful expansion of config surface. Whether this is justified by demonstrated client need or is speculative generality (YAGNI) is a judgment call; the plan does cite a broad, diverse client base as motivation, which is a plausible justification.

3. **Dual-layer architecture (Layer-1 + Layer-2)**: Two layers with distinct verdict models (deterministic-blocking vs. advisory-coaching) is a real design split. The plan argues the distinction is load-bearing (cheap/frozen/polyglot vs. LLM-judgment), so this does not appear gratuitous, but it does add a coordination seam that is only justified if the deterministic checks genuinely can't be expressed as advisory criteria with `blocking=true`. This is worth confirming in implementation.

4. **`REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event**: Introducing a new event type specifically for offline FP/remediation analysis (explicitly reducer-ignored) is a cheap observability investment the plan defends clearly. Not flagged as over-engineering.

5. **Polyglot tooling stack (solidlsp + ast-grep + syft + scc + lizard)**: Five tools for structural checks is non-trivial. The plan acknowledges solidlsp is already in the repo, which is a positive reuse signal. Whether ast-grep, syft, scc, and lizard are also already present or are net-new dependencies requires codebase confirmation. Adding four new heavyweight dependencies for checks that fail-open (skipped=PASS) could be disproportionate if simpler heuristics suffice for the majority of cases.

The primary ambiguity is whether the 'criteria registry + generic orchestrator' duplicates existing rebar.llm dispatch infrastructure, and whether the tooling dependencies (ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard) are already present. Neither can be resolved from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: In the AC or Design Summary, add an explicit statement such as: 'The criteria orchestrator extends/wraps [specific existing class/module] from rebar.llm — no new dispatch abstraction is introduced.' Similarly, note which of ast-grep/syft/scc/lizard are already repo dependencies vs. net-new, and confirm each net-new tool pulls its weight given fail-open semantics.

findings.location: Success Criteria + Acceptance Criteria sections; Design summary 'Execution' and 'Attestation' bullets
findings.finding:
The plan has a well-defined structural end-state (signed attestation blocks open→in_progress, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar emitted, Layer-1 + Layer-2 criteria implemented, docs shipped), but two reachability gaps make it ambiguous whether the union of described steps fully closes the acceptance criteria:

1. **Experiment-grounded defaults (AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results')** — The plan references 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' and links to a session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`), but no experiment sub-tasks or experiment deliverables are listed in the epic or follow-on work. It is unclear whether the experiments are assumed pre-existing, in-scope-but-undescribed, or out-of-scope. If they are not yet done, the AC cannot be verified at completion, and the agent has no stopping condition for this criterion.

2. **Centrality/blast-radius computation (AC: 'escalation by size+complexity+centrality (centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time)')** — The design mentions centrality is computed at plan time, but no step, tool, or data source for computing it is described. It is unknown whether this is covered by existing rebar infrastructure or requires new work. Without that, the escalation logic (and thus proportionate-depth routing) cannot be fully verified as reachable.

Neither gap is a showstopper by itself (both have plausible readings under which prior work already covers them), but the agent has no clear in-plan signal for when these two sub-outcomes are done.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. Either add a brief note that the chunking/budget/depth experiments are a prerequisite already completed (with a pointer to results), or create a scoped experiment sub-task and reference it explicitly from the AC so the agent knows when 'grounded in experiment results' is satisfied.
2. Add one sentence to the 'Execution' design bullet or a new AC clarifying how centrality/blast-radius is sourced (e.g., 'reuses rebar's existing dependency-graph pass', or 'new utility function over the file-impact list'). This gives the agent a concrete stopping condition for the escalation feature.

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment results)'
findings.finding:
The acceptance criterion for default chunking, budget, and depth values defers to an external artifact ('see linked experiment work') that is not included in or summarized within this plan. An executing agent cannot determine the actual numeric defaults (e.g., criteria-per-chunk, token budget cap, complexity thresholds) without access to that session log or the referenced experiments. This is a soft external dependency that could force an executor to stop and ask — or guess — at concrete values.

Additionally, two related ambiguities exist:
1. AC: 'escalation by size+complexity+centrality' — the thresholds or formulas that trigger escalation to a stronger model are not defined anywhere in the plan. 'Computed at plan time' is stated but the algorithm/metric is unspecified.
2. Design summary: 'chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB … default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' — same issue; the empirical default is referenced but not stated.
findings.suggested_edit:
Add a short 'Defaults (to be confirmed by experiment)' table in the plan with placeholder ranges, e.g.:
- Default chunk size: N criteria per LLM call (update after experiment run)
- Per-plan token budget cap: X tokens (update after experiment run)
- Complexity escalation threshold: score ≥ Y → stronger model
- Centrality/blast-radius definition: e.g., direct + transitive dependents count

This makes the plan self-contained and lets an executor proceed (or file explicit follow-on experiment tickets) rather than hunting an external session log.

findings.location: Design summary — multiple sections
findings.finding:
The plan makes several assertions about the existing codebase state that an executor would need to verify before relying on them:

1. **'solidlsp (already in repo)'** — The plan asserts solidlsp is already present and usable. An executor needs to confirm it is actually importable/integrated at the required version, not merely vendored or partially wired.

2. **'reusing rebar.llm extension points' / 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'** — The plan treats rebar.llm as already exposing a stable orchestration contract (catalog, contracts, prompt, runner). An executor must verify that these extension points exist, are stable, and are sufficient for the criteria-registry + generic-orchestrator design before building against them.

3. **'extends the close-gate signing'** (Acceptance Criteria — Attestation) — The plan assumes the close-gate signing mechanism is modular/reusable without modification. An executor needs to confirm the close-gate signing is factored such that it can be extended rather than duplicated.

4. **'ast-grep (resolution/callers)'** — Stated as available tooling without confirming it is installed/available in the execution environment or already declared as a dependency.

5. **'syft + registry + LLM-fallback (deps)'** and **'scc + lizard (size)'** — Similarly assumed available; no inventory of what is currently integrated vs. what must be added.

6. **'~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'** — Cited as a motivating statistic. If this comes from rebar's own telemetry, an executor should confirm the source; if from an external study, it may not apply to this specific project.

None of these can be confirmed or refuted from the plan text alone; they require inspection of the live codebase and dependency manifests.
findings.suggested_edit:
Add a short 'Pre-implementation verification checklist' section (or expand the Context section) that explicitly names each assumed-present component and directs the executor to confirm it before starting:
- Confirm solidlsp is importable at the required API surface.
- Confirm rebar.llm exposes catalog/contracts/prompt/runner as stable, documented extension points.
- Confirm the close-gate signing module is factored for reuse (not monolithic).
- Confirm ast-grep, syft, scc, and lizard are declared dependencies or are available in CI/dev environments.
This removes ambiguity and prevents mid-sprint discovery of missing scaffolding.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Criteria registry: each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)' and 'Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing … escalation by size+complexity+centrality … per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing' and 'Per-criterion config: advisory (default) | blocking-with-override'
findings.finding:
Three potential over-engineering / NIH concerns that cannot be fully resolved without access to the live codebase:

1. **Criteria-registry + generic orchestrator (potential NIH / YAGNI abstraction):** The plan calls for a new declarative criteria registry with a generic orchestrator, explicitly "reusing rebar.llm extension points". Whether this is a thin wrapper on an already-capable pipeline or a net-new abstraction layer needs confirmation. If rebar.llm's existing catalog/runner can already drive per-criterion prompts with minor parameterisation, a stand-alone registry object with its own descriptor schema could be a one-call-site abstraction that violates Rule-of-Three.

2. **Configuration surface proliferation:** The plan introduces at least six distinct project-configurable knobs: (a) per-criterion advisory vs. blocking, (b) chunking granularity, (c) budget cap, (d) review depth, (e) INDETERMINATE blocking default, and (f) escalation model selection. This is a non-trivial config surface for a first-ship feature. Without knowing how many of these map onto existing rebar project-config primitives vs. requiring new config schema, there is a real risk of gratuitous surface growth. The plan notes "sensible defaults shipped" but does not cap the surface or indicate which knobs collapse into a single risk-posture setting.

3. **Dual-layer architecture (potential premature abstraction):** Layer 1 (deterministic/blocking) and Layer 2 (advisory/LLM) are described as separate architectural layers with distinct routing, verdict models, and toolchains. If the only current consumer is this single gate, this two-layer split may be a Rule-of-Three violation at inception. A simpler first-ship model could be a single ordered list of criteria tagged `blocking: true/false` without a formal layer boundary — the layering could be introduced later once a second gate (e.g., the bug-ticket follow-on F6) reuses it.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. Add a one-line note confirming whether the criteria registry is a thin config dict passed to the existing rebar.llm runner or a net-new class with its own interface — if the former, drop the word "registry" to avoid implying more structure than exists.
2. Reduce the out-of-box config surface to a single `risk_posture: [permissive | standard | strict]` enum that bundles the most common knob combinations, with per-criterion overrides as an advanced escape hatch. This cuts the perceived proliferation without losing flexibility.
3. Explicitly state in the design summary that the Layer-1 / Layer-2 naming is a *conceptual* grouping of criteria by enforcement mode, not a class/module boundary requiring separate infrastructure — unless a concrete second consumer already exists that would reuse the split.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC item: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).'
findings.finding:
This AC is partially unmeasurable and out-of-session for an agent: it defers verification to an external experiment work item ('see linked experiment work') that is not defined within the plan itself. An agent cannot verify this criterion without access to the linked session log ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b') and the ~30 research reports referenced only by name. If those artifacts are not available in the agent's working context, this criterion cannot be completed or verified in-session.

A secondary concern: several ACs describe outcomes at a level that is hard to operationalize into a pass/fail check without further specification:
- 'Layer-2 advisory criteria... measurably help an agent revise the plan' (Success Criteria SC3) — 'measurably help' has no defined measurement protocol (no benchmark, no baseline, no threshold).
- 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec (grounded/specific/ranked/affirming/checklist)' — conformance is a judgment call unless there is a test suite or rubric with concrete pass/fail thresholds.
- 'Docs: how the gate works...' — completeness of documentation is subjective without a specific doc-coverage checklist.

None of these are blocking on their own, but together they represent a moderate risk of an agent shipping an implementation and being unable to self-verify several ACs.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. Either inline the relevant experiment results (e.g., 'default chunk size = N criteria/call, budget cap = X tokens, depth thresholds = [low/medium/high] for complexity scores [a, b, c]') directly into the AC, or replace the reference with a concrete, agent-verifiable stand-in such as 'defaults are documented in config with comments citing their empirical basis; experiment results are attached as docs/experiment_results.md'.
2. For 'measurably help an agent revise the plan', define a concrete proxy: e.g., 'at least one round-trip test case demonstrates that a flawed plan with seeded defects produces FAIL findings that, when addressed, yield PASS on re-review'.
3. For the coaching-spec conformance AC, add a reference to a specific rubric or test fixture (e.g., a golden-file test with a known-bad plan and expected finding structure) that an agent can run deterministically.

findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — final bullet: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).' and the follow-on list (F1, F10).
findings.finding:
The plan describes what will be built but is largely silent on how the gate itself will be tested. There is no explicit testing plan — no mention of unit tests, integration tests, behavioral/contractual coverage, failure-path coverage, or RED-before-GREEN ordering for the new code. The closest the plan comes is (a) referencing an experiment for setting defaults and (b) deferring FP/remediation analysis to follow-on F1/F10. Neither constitutes a testing plan for the implementation.

Specifically missing:
1. How Layer-1 deterministic checks are tested (e.g., do they have unit tests with stubbed LSP/ast-grep/syft responses? Are fail-open paths exercised with deliberately crashing or absent tools?).
2. How the attestation signing, invalidation-on-material-edit, and `claim` fast-check are exercised (boundary: what counts as a 'material edit'? Is there a test that edits a tag vs. a description and verifies only the latter invalidates?).
3. How the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event / reducer-skip is verified (a structural grep or event-log assertion would suffice, but it isn't mentioned).
4. Whether Layer-2 advisory criteria outputs are tested for coaching-spec conformance (grounded / severity-ranked / checklist-form).
5. No mention of failure-path tests: budget exhaustion → INDETERMINATE, tool crash → skipped=PASS, unsupported language → PASS.

This is AMBIGUOUS rather than FAIL because the plan is an epic-level description referencing a detailed session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) that may contain a testing plan not reproduced here. The epic itself, however, must be self-contained per its own stated intent ('This description is the self-contained epic summary'), so a testing plan should appear here or in the ACs.
findings.suggested_edit:
Add a dedicated 'Testing approach' subsection (or an AC bullet) covering at minimum:
- Layer-1 unit tests: one positive + one fail-open (tool-unavailable) case per structural check.
- Layer-2 integration test: a synthetic ticket with known plan defects; assert advisory findings are emitted with correct severity/grounding.
- Attestation boundary tests: material-edit (description change) invalidates cert; non-material edit (tag change) does not.
- `claim` fast-check: valid cert → proceeds; absent cert → blocks; stale cert → blocks; `--force` override → allowed with justification logged.
- Failure-path: budget exhaustion → INDETERMINATE (non-blocking); tool crash/timeout → skipped=PASS.
- `REVIEW_RESULT` event: reducer-skip verified (e.g., structural assertion that the reducer ignores the event type).
Explicitly note RED-before-GREEN intent for the `claim` integration path.

findings.location: AC item: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' vs. Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'
findings.finding:
Three issues found, all minor in isolation but worth addressing together:

1. **Orphan AC with unresolved external dependency:** AC item 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' references experiment work that is not described anywhere in the plan body and not listed in the Success Criteria. The plan body says defaults are 'set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' but gives no detail on where those experiments live, who runs them, or when they complete relative to this epic. If the experiment work is a prerequisite, it should be a named dependency or follow-on; if it is in-scope, it needs a description. As written it is an orphan AC with a dangling external reference that an agent cannot resolve.

2. **Near-duplicate coverage language:** The Design summary states 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' in two separate bullets (Execution and Attestation sections), and AC item 6 ('Configurable chunking … escalation by size+complexity+centrality (centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time)') repeats this verbatim. These are not inconsistent, but the repetition without added detail risks an implementer treating them as separate deliverables. A single canonical location with references elsewhere would be cleaner.

3. **Terminology near-duplicate — 'coaching' posture:** The Design summary uses 'COACH' (all-caps) in the Context section and 'coaching' (lower-case) in the Coaching/feedback spec and AC item 3 ('Layer-2 review returns coaching findings … checklist-form'). The Success Criteria item 3 uses 'coaching findings'. This is consistent enough to be benign, but the all-caps emphasis in Context vs. lower-case elsewhere could mislead an agent into thinking 'COACH' is a system component name rather than a posture description. A brief parenthetical like 'COACH (advisory posture)' on first use would disambiguate.

No described work lacks a criterion and no criterion is wholly orphaned from the plan body, so the overall criterion is met with the caveat above on the experiment-results AC.
findings.suggested_edit:
For issue 1: Either (a) add a named prerequisite ticket for the experiment work and reference it explicitly ('Defaults are imported from experiment ticket #XXX, a prerequisite'), or (b) add a brief in-scope description of the experiment procedure in the Design summary and a corresponding Success Criterion, or (c) move the AC item to Follow-on work as F13 if experiments cannot be completed within this epic.

For issue 2: Remove the duplicate '(centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time)' from the Attestation bullet and keep it only in the Execution bullet, adding a cross-reference: 'centrality computed at plan time (see Execution)'.

For issue 3: On first use in Context, change 'COACH' to 'coach (advisory posture)' to signal it is a posture description, not a component name.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python … file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep)' and Design Summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep … syft + registry + LLM-fallback (deps); scc + lizard (size)'
findings.finding: The plan names a handful of tooling components that are already in the repo (solidlsp, rebar.llm catalog/contracts/prompt/runner, the completion-verifier signing machinery) and correctly acknowledges that those must be extended, but it does NOT enumerate the specific files/modules/symbols that will actually be created or modified. There is no list such as: 'extend `claim` command in <module>; add `REVIEW_RESULT` event type to the reducer skip-list in <module>; add criteria registry in <new module>; extend attestation signer from <existing module>'. Without that mapping the blast-radius of this change — across the event reducer, the claim workflow, the signing/attestation subsystem, the rebar.llm orchestrator, and presumably a new plan-review package — cannot be verified from the plan text alone. Whether the named existing modules actually exist at the paths implied, and whether undiscovered callers of the claim or attestation code need updating, requires access to the live codebase.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / modules touched' subsection (even a rough table) that lists: (a) existing files being extended (e.g., the claim command, the reducer event-type skip-list, the attestation signer, the rebar.llm orchestrator entry point), (b) net-new files/packages to be created (e.g., `plan_review/`, `plan_review/criteria_registry.py`, `plan_review/layer1/`, `plan_review/layer2/`), and (c) any config-schema files that gain new keys. Explicitly note that callers of the `claim` API and the attestation validator will need to handle the new blocking behaviour. This keeps the blast-radius acknowledged rather than implicit.

findings.location: Multiple sections: Design summary ('two layers', 'routing pivots on has_children + complexity'), AC ('Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'), Follow-on F11 ('rigor-signature chain / CI integration helpers')
findings.finding:
Overall coherence is strong — nearly every AC item traces back to a named concept in the Design summary, and vice-versa. However, three specific gaps and one terminology inconsistency are worth flagging:

1. **Orphan AC item (no design coverage):** AC bullet 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' requires experiment results to exist and be available, but neither the Design summary nor the Success Criteria mention where those experiments live, who runs them, or whether they are in-scope for this epic. The linked session log is referenced but not findable externally. If the experiments are a prerequisite artifact, this dependency is implicit and could block implementation.

2. **Described work with thin/no AC:** The Design summary calls out 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' as a distinct computation step that drives escalation routing, but no AC bullet explicitly covers the correctness or contract of that computation (AC 6 mentions 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' only parenthetically as part of the escalation bullet). If this computation is non-trivial, its acceptance condition is underspecified.

3. **Follow-on F11 ('rigor-signature chain / CI integration helpers') vs. Success Criterion 6 ('The signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process'):** SC6 implies CI-checkability is in-scope and done, but F11 defers 'CI integration helpers' to follow-on. AC bullet 'Attestation: signing + … (extends the close-gate signing)' does not mention CI-checkability either. There is a mild contradiction: SC6 reads as a success criterion of this epic, yet the tooling to fulfil it is explicitly deferred.

4. **Terminology inconsistency — 'INDETERMINATE' vs. 'non-blocking default':** The Design summary introduces INDETERMINATE as a review-level verdict ('budget/tool-unavailable') and states it is 'non-blocking by default (project-configurable)'. AC bullet 6 repeats this correctly. However, the Success Criteria do not mention INDETERMINATE at all, while AC bullet 6 mentions it only in the escalation/budget-cap sentence. A reader building the per-criterion config knob could conflate INDETERMINATE (a review-level budget outcome) with AMBIGUOUS (a per-criterion verdict) — the two are distinct but the plan uses 'AMBIGUOUS escalates to a higher-tier adjudicator' in the Design summary without making the scope boundary (criterion-level vs. review-level) explicit in the AC.

No duplicate requirements were found; the Layer-1 and Layer-2 criterion lists are distinct and non-overlapping.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. For the experiment-results dependency: add an explicit AC bullet such as 'Empirical experiments establishing default chunking/budget/depth values are completed and their outputs are committed to the repo before this epic ships; the gate defaults reference those outputs directly.'
2. For centrality/blast-radius: expand AC bullet 6 to explicitly state what 'computed at plan time' means (e.g., 'centrality is derived from the ticket's file_impact set and the repo call-graph, producing a numeric score used to select escalation model tier') so it has a testable contract.
3. For SC6 vs. F11: either (a) move SC6 to Follow-on and remove it from this epic's Success Criteria, or (b) add a minimal AC bullet covering what CI-checkable means in-scope here (e.g., 'attestation can be verified by a standalone CLI command with a zero exit code, sufficient for a CI step') and clarify that richer integration helpers are F11.
4. For INDETERMINATE/AMBIGUOUS: add a one-sentence definitional note in the AC preamble distinguishing the two levels: 'AMBIGUOUS is a per-criterion verdict; INDETERMINATE is a review-level outcome when budget or tooling prevents completing the review.'

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all 12 ACs) and Design Summary
findings.finding: This epic is scoped as a single executable unit but encompasses a very large, multi-layered body of work that cannot coherently be executed in one agent session. The plan bundles at least the following distinct vertical slices into a single ticket with no child decomposition: (1) Layer-1 deterministic infrastructure (LSP/ast-grep/syft/scc/lizard integration, fail-open wiring, 7+ individual structural checks); (2) Layer-2 advisory LLM review engine (11+ named criteria, coaching-spec formatting, per-criterion config knob); (3) Criteria registry + generic orchestrator (new declarative descriptor system, reusing rebar.llm); (4) Attestation subsystem (signing, material-event-commit binding, invalidation logic); (5) `claim` integration (fast-path check, --force override + justification); (6) Configurable routing/chunking/budget/escalation/centrality engine; (7) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip + fingerprint emission; (8) Experiment-grounded defaults (implies experiments must be run first or already done); (9) Documentation (gate behavior, config surface, cert semantics, CI-check pattern). Each of these is independently releasable and testable. The plan itself calls out a rich set of follow-on tickets (F1–F12) but provides zero child decomposition for the in-scope work. The design summary references 'routing pivots', 'chunk-atomic checkpointing', 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time', and a multi-model escalation path — each a non-trivial implementation unit. No child tickets are listed or referenced.
