This creates a high risk that an agent starting execution will either (a) deliver a shallow, incomplete implementation across all layers, or (b) make arbitrary scoping decisions mid-flight that diverge from the plan's intent.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose this epic into at least the following child tickets before claiming it: C1 — Layer-1 structural floor (tooling wrappers + fail-open harness + each deterministic check); C2 — Criteria registry + generic orchestrator; C3 — Layer-2 advisory criteria engine (LLM runner, per-criterion verdict model, coaching-spec formatter); C4 — Attestation subsystem (signing, material-event binding, invalidation); C5 — `claim` integration (fast-path attestation check, --force override); C6 — Routing/chunking/budget/escalation/centrality engine; C7 — REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event (reducer skip, fingerprint schema, override logging); C8 — Experiment suite for default-tuning (if not already done, gate C6 on this); C9 — Documentation and CI-check pattern. The parent epic ticket should track these children and define only the cross-cutting constraints (fail-open contract, coaching spec, attestation schema) rather than carrying executable ACs itself.

findings.location: ## Context, paragraph 1 and 2
Acceptance Criteria section (all 12 bullet points) and 'Follow-on work' section
findings.finding: This ticket is framed as an epic but has NO decomposition into child tickets within the epic itself. All 12 acceptance criteria are bundled into a single unit of work, spanning: (1) two distinct runtime layers (Layer-1 deterministic + Layer-2 advisory) each containing 8–11 individual criteria; (2) a criteria registry + generic orchestrator; (3) an attestation subsystem with signing, invalidation logic, and material-event binding; (4) `claim` command integration; (5) configurable chunking/routing/escalation/budget machinery; (6) a `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event + reducer skip; (7) per-criterion config knobs; (8) experiment-grounded defaults (implying the experiments themselves must be run or already exist); and (9) documentation. These are at least 6–8 independently deliverable vertical slices, each of which alone would be a multi-session effort. The plan explicitly defers 12 follow-on items (F1–F12) but does not decompose the epic's own scope into child tickets. An agent attempting to execute this as a single leaf ticket will either one-shot an under-specified blob or stall trying to decide scope boundaries mid-execution—exactly the failure mode the gate itself is designed to prevent.
findings.suggested_edit:
File child tickets (linked to this epic) for at minimum the following vertical slices, each with its own AC:
1. Layer-1 deterministic floor (readiness shape, LSP/ast-grep resolution, package existence, oversize, DAG validity, AC/DD quality, destructive sniff) — polyglot fail-open implementation.
2. Layer-2 advisory criteria implementation (the ~11 named criteria + overlays + broad pass).
3. Criteria registry + generic orchestrator (declarative descriptors, reusing rebar.llm extension points).
4. Attestation subsystem (signing, material-event-commit binding, invalidation, coverage record).
5. `claim` integration (fast signed-attestation check, `--force`+justification override).
6. Routing, chunking, escalation, and budget machinery (complexity+has_children router, centrality/blast-radius, per-plan budget cap, chunk-atomic checkpointing, INDETERMINATE outcome).
7. `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event (reducer skip, fingerprints, override logging).
8. Experiment runs to ground chunking/budget/depth defaults (prerequisite for item 6).
9. Docs.
Each child ticket should own its own success/acceptance criteria and carry a reference back to this epic.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).'
findings.finding:
Most of the acceptance criteria are well-scoped and verifiable (e.g., Layer-1 criteria are enumerated, Layer-2 criteria are enumerated, attestation signing is described, `claim` integration is concrete, sidecar event structure is named). However, two areas create ambiguity about whether the described steps fully reach the stated end-state:

1. **Experiment dependency (AC bullet 11):** The AC requires that 'defaults for chunking + budget + depth [are] grounded in the experiment results', but the experiments themselves are not in scope for this epic — they are referenced only as 'linked experiment work'. There is no acceptance criterion that defines what constitutes a passing experiment result, who runs the experiments, or how the outputs are translated into concrete default values before this epic closes. Without those steps being part of the plan, the gap between 'what we'll do' and 'what done looks like' for this AC cannot be closed within the epic's own boundary.

2. **Docs AC (bullet 12):** 'Docs: how the gate works, the config surface, fail-open behavior, the cert semantics, and the CI-check pattern.' This is stated as an AC bullet but there is no corresponding step in the Design Summary or plan body that describes who writes the docs, in what form (e.g., markdown file, wiki page, inline docstrings), or what 'done' looks like for each topic. The termination condition for this bullet is unclear.

These are not blocking gaps — the core functional work (Layers 1 & 2, attestation, `claim` gate, sidecar event) has clear enough end-states — but the two items above lack a verifiable termination signal within the epic.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. For the experiment AC: either (a) explicitly scope the experiments as a sub-task within this epic with a concrete deliverable (e.g., 'an experiment ticket is linked and its results artifact is attached before this epic closes'), or (b) change the AC to 'sensible hard-coded defaults are shipped and will be tuned by follow-on experiment work (F-experiment)' so the AC is achievable without an external dependency.

2. For the docs AC: add a termination condition, e.g., 'A `docs/plan-review-gate.md` file is present covering: gate trigger/flow, config surface (with all keys and defaults), fail-open semantics, attestation/cert semantics, and CI-check pattern; each section has at least one worked example.'

findings.location: Design summary — multiple sections
findings.finding:
The plan makes several assertions about the existing codebase that an executor would need to verify against the live repo before relying on them:

1. **"solidlsp (already in repo)"** — The plan states solidlsp is already present in the repository. If it is not, or is an incompatible version, the Layer-1 LSP/ast-grep resolution criteria cannot be implemented as described.

2. **"reusing rebar.llm extension points" / "reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner"** — The plan assumes rebar.llm already exposes a generic orchestrator API (catalog, contracts, prompt, runner) compatible with a criteria-registry pattern. If the extension points don't exist or have a different interface, the orchestrator design needs revision.

3. **"extends the close-gate signing"** (Attestation AC) — The plan assumes a signing mechanism already exists in the completion-verifier close gate and that it is extensible to the open→in_progress flow. If the close-gate signing is tightly coupled to its own flow or not abstracted, reuse may require significant refactoring not accounted for in scope.

4. **"syft + registry + LLM-fallback (deps)"** / **"scc + lizard (size)"** — These tools are listed as if already available or easily integrated. Their presence in the environment (installed, accessible, version-compatible) is unverified in the plan.

5. **"~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects"** — Cited as motivation. The source is not referenced beyond 'session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' which reviewers and executors cannot access. If this figure is wrong, it weakens prioritisation arguments, though it does not affect implementation directly.

None of these can be confirmed or refuted without access to the live codebase and environment.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief 'Prerequisites / Verified assumptions' subsection that explicitly lists: (a) solidlsp is present at path X and version Y; (b) rebar.llm exposes the orchestrator extension points needed (link to the relevant module/interface); (c) the close-gate signing abstraction and its reuse surface; (d) syft, scc, lizard, and ast-grep availability in the execution environment. Flag any that are unconfirmed so the executor knows to verify them first.

findings.location: ## Success Criteria / ## Acceptance Criteria — no dedicated 'Testing' section exists anywhere in the plan
findings.finding:
The plan is entirely silent on how the gate itself will be tested. There is no mention of a test suite, test strategy, behavioral coverage, failure-path cases, or RED-before-GREEN ordering for any of the implementation deliverables (Layer-1 checks, Layer-2 criteria, attestation signing, claim integration, sidecar event, config surface, etc.). The plan describes *what* to build in detail but leaves the question 'how do we know it works correctly?' completely unaddressed.

Specific gaps:
1. **Layer-1 deterministic checks** — no mention of unit tests for each structural check (e.g., a ticket with a missing `## Acceptance Criteria` section should trigger readiness-shape failure; a ticket referencing a non-existent file should be flagged). Boundary cases (file exists vs. doesn't exist; LSP times out; unsupported language) need explicit coverage.
2. **Fail-open / polyglot behavior** — the plan states 'unsupported lang / no server / crash / timeout → skipped = PASS, never FAIL' (Design summary) but provides no test cases or contract tests verifying this invariant holds under each failure mode.
3. **Layer-2 advisory criteria** — no mention of golden-set / snapshot tests, prompt-regression harness, or any mechanism to detect when a criterion's LLM prompt regresses.
4. **Attestation / signing** — no tests described for: valid signature accepted, stale/invalidated signature rejected, material vs. non-material edit classification.
5. **`claim` integration** — no contract or integration test described for the fast-check path, `--force` override path, or absence-of-attestation path.
6. **REVIEW_RESULT sidecar** — no test verifying the reducer ignores the event type and that the event payload is offline-analysis-ready.
7. **Experiment-grounded defaults (AC bullet 11)** — the plan defers defaults to experiment results but does not describe how those experiments will be run or what 'grounded' means in a testable sense.

Because the plan is an epic-level description (not yet a code PR), it is *possible* the author intends testing to be specified at child-ticket level — but nothing in the plan states this, and the AC list does not include a 'tests written' criterion.
findings.suggested_edit:
Add a dedicated **Testing strategy** section (or AC bullet) that covers at minimum:
- Unit tests for every Layer-1 check with at least one PASS, one FAIL, and one fail-open (tool-unavailable) case per check.
- Contract/integration tests for the `claim` fast-check path: (a) valid attestation → proceeds, (b) absent attestation → blocks, (c) stale/material-edited attestation → blocks, (d) `--force` → overrides and logs.
- A golden-set regression harness (even a small hand-curated set of tickets with known expected verdicts) to guard Layer-2 prompt quality.
- Explicit statement that fail-open behavior is tested via mock/stubbed tool failures (crash, timeout, unsupported language).
- RED-before-GREEN ordering: tests are written before (or alongside) each Layer-1 check implementation, as a sub-requirement of the relevant child ticket.
- Reducer-ignorance of `REVIEW_RESULT` verified by an event-sourcing replay test.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)': solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep + syft + registry + LLM-fallback + scc + lizard; 'Criteria registry: declarative descriptor + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points'; 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding'; 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event'
findings.finding:
Several design choices could represent over-engineering or NIH depending on what already exists in rebar — which cannot be confirmed from plan text alone:

1. **Tooling stack breadth (solidlsp + ast-grep + syft + scc + lizard + registry + LLM-fallback):** The plan explicitly notes solidlsp is already in the repo, which is the right signal to look for. Whether ast-grep, syft, scc, and lizard are already dependencies or need to be introduced is unknown. Introducing five new binary/library dependencies purely for a plan-review gate could be over-engineering if simpler heuristics (line-count from git, import lists from the ticket text) would satisfy the fail-open polyglot floor at a fraction of the integration cost.

2. **Criteria registry + generic orchestrator:** The plan calls for 'a generic orchestrator running declarative criteria descriptors, reusing rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner.' If rebar.llm already has a prompt-runner and the registry is a thin config file, this is fine reuse. However, if the 'declarative descriptor' schema, the registry loader, the orchestrator dispatch loop, and the chunking knob are all new code built specifically for this one feature, that is a new abstraction layer used at exactly one call-site — a Rule-of-Three violation.

3. **Attestation / signing subsystem:** The plan says this 'extends the close-gate signing.' If the close-gate already has a signing utility, reuse is appropriate and not NIH. But the addition of 'material-event-commit binding,' 'coverage record,' and 'invalidation-on-material-edit' logic may meaningfully expand the signing surface beyond what a simple token/hash would require. Whether that expansion is already present in the close-gate or is net-new complexity needs codebase confirmation.

4. **Configuration proliferation:** The plan introduces at minimum: per-criterion advisory/blocking knob, chunking granularity knob, budget-cap knob, depth/complexity routing knob, centrality/blast-radius knob, INDETERMINATE non-blocking default knob, and model-escalation knob. That is 6–7 new project-level config axes for a single gate. The plan acknowledges 'sensible defaults' but does not explain why all seven axes need to be independently tunable at launch vs. being hardcoded/constant until empirical data (from the linked experiments) justifies exposing them. This is a real configuration-surface proliferation risk.

None of these are definitively over-engineering from plan text alone — the plan explicitly invokes reuse at several points — but the combination warrants a concrete audit: confirm which tools are already in-repo, confirm the close-gate signing is actually extended (not duplicated), and consider deferring config knobs that are not required by any stated acceptance criterion until F-series follow-ons provide empirical justification.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief 'reuse inventory' bullet to the Design Summary explicitly naming: (a) which of ast-grep/syft/scc/lizard are already repo dependencies vs. new introductions; (b) which close-gate signing primitives are being reused verbatim vs. extended; (c) which config knobs are required at launch (i.e., referenced by a concrete AC) vs. aspirational (candidates for follow-on). This makes NIH/over-engineering risks visible before implementation and prevents the config surface from expanding by default.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Layer-1 and Layer-2 bullets; Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling' and 'Execution' sections
findings.finding: The plan lists specific tooling (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, lizard, scc) and names the generic orchestrator and criteria registry as new artefacts, but no specific file paths, module names, or package boundaries are given for any of them. Consumers of the 'claim' command that will need updating (the open→in_progress transition handler, the reducer, any CLI entry-points) are mentioned only abstractly ('claim integration'). Without codebase access it is impossible to confirm whether those call-sites exist, whether the existing close-gate signing code is the correct reuse point, or whether 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' implies touching dependency-graph infrastructure that is not currently listed as an edit target. The blast radius is acknowledged at a high level ('extends the close-gate signing') but no concrete list of files-to-touch is provided, which is typical for an epic-level ticket yet still leaves ambiguity about undocumented callers.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / modules in scope' subsection (even a rough list) naming: (a) the existing close-gate signing module to be extended, (b) the claim/transition handler to be patched, (c) the reducer module that must learn to skip REVIEW_RESULT events, and (d) the new modules that will house the criteria registry and orchestrator. Flag any dependency-graph or centrality-computation infrastructure as a known additional edit target.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Criteria registry: each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly calls out reuse of rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner machinery, which is a good anti-NIH signal. However, it also introduces a new 'criteria registry' with 'declarative descriptors', a new 'generic orchestrator', a new sidecar event type (REVIEW_RESULT), a new per-criterion config knob (advisory | blocking-with-override), and a new signing/attestation extension — all in one epic. Without seeing the live codebase it is impossible to confirm whether equivalent registry/orchestrator or config-knob patterns already exist in rebar (e.g. from the completion-verifier) that could be reused rather than re-invented. If the completion-verifier already has a criteria registry and orchestrator, building a parallel one here would be NIH. The plan does not explicitly state whether the completion-verifier's orchestration layer will be shared or duplicated.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Reuse vs. new' note explicitly stating which parts of the completion-verifier's orchestration, signing, and config infrastructure are being extended vs. newly built, to rule out inadvertent duplication.

findings.location: N/A

Acceptance Criteria — 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python … file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep)'; Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp … + ast-grep'; 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time'
findings.finding:
The plan names several concrete tool integrations (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, lizard, scc) and mentions that 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' is part of the scope, but it does not enumerate the specific source files, modules, or symbols within the rebar codebase that will be created or modified to implement this feature. Concretely:

1. **New files/modules** — the plan describes new components (criteria registry, orchestrator, attestation signer, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, claim integration, config surface, docs) but never names the expected file paths (e.g., `rebar/plan_review/`, `rebar/claim.py`, etc.). Without file-level targets it is impossible to assess scope accuracy or confirm no existing callers are silently broken.

2. **Modified files/modules** — the plan states that `claim` gains a 'fast signed-attestation check' and that the close-gate signing is extended, but does not identify the existing modules that will be changed (e.g., the existing claim handler, the completion-verifier, the reducer). These are high-blast-radius touch points whose current callers/consumers are unknown from the plan text alone.

3. **Blast-radius acknowledgement** — the plan acknowledges the concept of blast-radius for the *reviewed* tickets (as a criterion computed at plan time), but does not acknowledge the blast radius of the *implementation itself* (e.g., 'reducer skips REVIEW_RESULT type' implies a reducer change; 'claim integration' implies changes to an existing critical path command; 'extends the close-gate signing' implies changes to a shared signing module). These cross-cutting change points are not named, so downstream callers of the claim path or close-gate are unverifiable from the plan.

4. **External consumers** — since rebar is described as serving a 'broad, diverse client base', any change to the `claim` command API or event schema (new `REVIEW_RESULT` event type, new attestation fields) may require updates to client integrations, CI scripts, or documentation. No such consumers are mentioned.

This is AMBIGUOUS rather than a hard FAIL because (a) the plan explicitly defers implementation-level file decomposition to linked sub-tickets ('follow-on work to be filed as separate linked tickets'), so it may be intentionally abstract at the epic level; and (b) without access to the live codebase the reviewer cannot confirm whether named tools are already integrated or whether the existing claim/close-gate modules are the only touch points.
findings.suggested_edit:
Add a 'Files / Modules Changed' subsection to the Design Summary (or to each Acceptance Criterion where applicable) listing, at minimum:
- The new top-level package(s) to be created (e.g., `rebar/plan_review/` with its sub-modules: `registry.py`, `orchestrator.py`, `layer1/`, `layer2/`, `attestation.py`, `sidecar.py`).
- The existing files being modified (e.g., the `claim` command handler, the reducer event-type allowlist, the close-gate signing module, any shared config loader).
- A one-line blast-radius note for the `claim` integration (e.g., 'Only the open→in_progress transition path is affected; read-only paths are untouched') so reviewers and future agents can confirm scope without codebase access.

If this epic intentionally omits file-level details because they belong to child tickets, say so explicitly and confirm that each child ticket will carry a concrete edit-set.

findings.location: Design summary – 'Execution' bullet; AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: Several design decisions are deferred to experiment results or a linked session log rather than stated in the plan itself. Specifically: (1) 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' for criteria-per-LLM-call chunking — the executor must find and interpret external experiment data before choosing a default; (2) 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' points to work that has not been filed/linked as a child ticket, so an executor cannot locate it deterministically; (3) 'see linked experiment work' implies the experiments are a prerequisite but no ticket link or result summary is embedded in the plan. These are real decision-deferrals that may cause an agent to stall or guess. The session-log reference ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b') is the single source of supplementary design truth, but it is external and inaccessible to a reviewer without repo access, so it cannot be confirmed to resolve all ambiguities.
findings.suggested_edit: Inline the chosen defaults (or a concrete range with a rationale) for chunking, per-plan budget cap, and depth tiers directly in the AC or a 'Defaults' subsection. If the experiment work is truly a prerequisite, file it as a blocking dependency ticket and reference it by ticket ID. Remove or replace 'see linked experiment work' with either the actual numbers or an explicit 'TBD—blocked on ticket #X'.

findings.location: Title, Context, Design summary, Success Criteria, Acceptance Criteria
Acceptance Criteria (all 12 bullet points); Success Criteria (all 6 bullet points)
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan whatsoever. There is no mention of: (1) unit tests for the deterministic Layer-1 checks (e.g., file-resolution, oversize, DAG-validity logic); (2) integration or end-to-end tests for the open→in_progress gate flow (claim blocked/allowed, --force override path); (3) tests for attestation signing, material-edit invalidation, and staleness detection; (4) failure-path / fail-open behavior verification (unsupported lang → skip → PASS); (5) tests for the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event emission and reducer-skip behavior; (6) any RED-before-GREEN ordering guidance. The plan is feature-rich and detailed on architecture, but entirely silent on how any of it is verified beyond the behavioral success criteria themselves. For a gate that is safety-critical in a CI context, the absence of a test strategy is a significant gap that will cause rework.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Testing Plan' section (or sub-bullets under each AC) covering at minimum: (a) unit tests for each Layer-1 deterministic check with at least one passing and one fail-open (unsupported-stack) case; (b) integration test(s) for the full open→in_progress flow asserting blocking, --force bypass, and stale-signature rejection; (c) a fixture/mock strategy for LSP/ast-grep/syft to keep tests hermetic; (d) a test asserting the reducer ignores REVIEW_RESULT events; (e) a test confirming material vs. non-material edit invalidation. Structural command-output assertions are acceptable as cross-language coverage for polyglot checks.

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' and AC: 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented: ... intent fidelity (2-step blind-restate)'
findings.finding: Two acceptance criteria depend on external artifacts that are not self-contained in the plan and may not be reachable within a single working session. (1) 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' references a linked experiment work item; if those experiments haven't been run yet, the agent cannot complete this AC without running the experiments first — which is either out-of-scope work or an implicit ordering dependency that isn't stated. (2) 'Intent fidelity (2-step blind-restate)' is described but the acceptance criterion gives no measurable threshold (e.g. what pass/fail condition terminates the check?). Similarly, 'AC/DD quality' in Layer-1 and 'anti-slop' in Layer-2 are named but never given a measurable criterion for what constitutes pass vs. fail. These risk being 'works well'-style criteria that are hard to verify deterministically.
findings.suggested_edit: For the experiment-dependency AC, either (a) add a child ticket / prerequisite gate for the experiments and mark this AC blocked-on that ticket, or (b) specify that interim placeholder defaults are acceptable if experiments are not yet complete, with a follow-on to update them. For 'intent fidelity', 'anti-slop', and 'AC/DD quality', add a concrete measurable condition (e.g. 'restate divergence triggers an AMBIGUOUS verdict', 'slop patterns defined in registry config file', etc.).

findings.location: Context section: 'rebar has a completion-verifier close gate... It has no equivalent at the start of work'; 'Its posture is to COACH agents to author better plans via positive, well-formed guidance'; '~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'

Multiple locations: Design summary 'Routing pivots on has_children + complexity, NOT ticket type' vs. AC 'proportionate-depth routing (complexity + has_children)'; Success Criteria vs. Acceptance Criteria overlap; Follow-on F11 'rigor-signature chain / CI integration helpers' vs. AC 'The signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process'
findings.finding: Three coherence issues: (1) **Orphan AC with no description coverage**: The AC item 'Docs: how the gate works, the config surface, fail-open behavior, the cert semantics, and the CI-check pattern' has no corresponding description section in the plan body; documentation deliverables are entirely absent from the Design Summary and Success Criteria. (2) **Near-duplicate between Success Criteria and Acceptance Criteria**: Success Criterion 6 ('The signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process as a rigor/anti-vibe-coding signal') substantially overlaps with the AC on attestation signing and also with Follow-on F11 ('rigor-signature chain / CI integration helpers') — it is ambiguous whether the CI-check pattern is in-scope for this epic or deferred. (3) **Terminology inconsistency**: The plan alternates between 'attestation', 'signed attestation', 'certification', 'cert', and 'signature' to refer to the same artifact. While context makes this mostly clear, 'certification' is used in the Context section and 'attestation' everywhere else — a future agent may treat these as distinct objects.
findings.suggested_edit: (1) Add a 'Documentation' subsection to the Design Summary listing what docs are required, then ensure the AC doc item maps back to it. (2) Clarify whether Success Criterion 6 (CI-checkable attestation) is in-scope here or deferred to F11; if in-scope, ensure it has a corresponding AC beyond just 'attestation signing'. (3) Pick one canonical term (suggest 'attestation') and replace 'certification'/'cert' throughout.

findings.location: Success Criteria and Acceptance Criteria sections
findings.finding: The plan describes the end-state at a high level (gate fires, attestation signed, sidecar emitted, CI-checkable), but the verification method for the gate itself is not spelled out. Specifically: (1) There is no stated integration-test or end-to-end scenario that confirms open→in_progress is actually blocked when the attestation is absent or stale — only that the claim 'requires' it. (2) 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' defers a key input to 'see linked experiment work', which may not be complete before implementation starts, creating a potential stopping ambiguity. (3) The AC for Layer-2 ('coaching findings … measurably help an agent revise the plan') uses the word 'measurably' but no measurement instrument is defined here — that is deferred to Follow-on F1. This is internally consistent (FP tracking is explicitly a follow-on) but means the Layer-2 AC as written cannot be verified within this epic's scope. These are not blockers given the epic framing, but they should be acknowledged so the agent knows what 'done' does and does not include for Layer-2 quality.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a concrete verification scenario to the Acceptance Criteria, e.g.: 'An integration test asserts that `claim` on an open ticket without a valid attestation returns an error, and that `claim --force <justification>` succeeds and records the override.' Also clarify that the 'measurably' qualifier in the Layer-2 AC is verified by the REVIEW_RESULT event structure (not by measured remediation rates, which are F1 follow-on).

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (12 bullet items) + Design Summary (all subsections)
findings.finding: This is presented as an epic, so child decomposition is expected and the plan explicitly acknowledges it ('filed as separate linked tickets') — but the children are NOT filed here and the AC explicitly defers them all to follow-on (F1–F12). The core scope remaining in this single epic is still extremely large for one autonomous agent session: it includes (a) Layer-1 deterministic checks with 7+ distinct sub-checks (LSP/ast-grep integration, syft+registry dep checking, oversize signals, DAG validity, AC/DD quality, destructive sniff), (b) Layer-2 with ~12 advisory criteria plus router-selected overlays and a broad pass, (c) a criteria registry + generic orchestrator, (d) attestation signing + material-commit binding + invalidation logic, (e) `claim` integration, (f) configurable chunking + depth routing + budget caps + checkpointing, (g) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer exemption + override logging, (h) per-criterion blocking config, and (i) docs. None of the 12 AC items is further decomposed into child tickets within this plan. The plan acknowledges it is an epic but does not show child tickets that break this into executable units. A single agent session attempting all 12 AC items is extremely likely to fail or produce shallow implementations across many areas.
findings.suggested_edit: File child tickets (or at minimum enumerate them in this plan) that decompose the epic into independently executable vertical slices, e.g.: (1) Layer-1 structural floor + fail-open tooling harness, (2) Attestation signing + material-event-commit binding + `claim` integration, (3) Layer-2 criteria registry + orchestrator + coaching feedback spec, (4) Configurable chunking/routing/budget/checkpointing, (5) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + reducer exemption, (6) Docs + CI-check pattern. Each child should have its own AC narrow enough for one session.

findings.location: Design Summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)' and 'Criteria registry' paragraphs; Context paragraph ('reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner')
findings.finding: The plan rests on several unverified assertions about the existing codebase that an executor would need to confirm before relying on them: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' — the plan asserts solidlsp is present; if it is not integrated or is at an incompatible version, the LSP-based resolution layer breaks. (2) 'ast-grep (resolution/callers)' — assumed to be available in the toolchain; not confirmed. (3) 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' — assumes these extension points exist in their current form and are sufficient for a new gate; if the completion-verifier uses a different internal API shape, significant adaptation may be needed. (4) 'extends the close-gate signing' — assumes the close-gate signing module is factored in a way that supports extension rather than requiring duplication. (5) 'reducer-IGNORED SIDECAR `REVIEW_RESULT` event' — assumes the reducer architecture supports an ignorable event type without modification; not confirmed against the actual reducer. (6) 'Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' — the plan-of-record is external; if that log is unavailable or diverged, the executor lacks grounding for many design decisions. These are ambiguous because the plan cannot be verified against the live codebase from this review.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Preconditions / assumptions to verify' section that lists each assumption explicitly: solidlsp integration status, ast-grep availability, rebar.llm extension-point API surface, close-gate signing factoring, and reducer ignorable-event support. Link to the relevant source files or note where the executor must verify before proceeding. This converts implicit assumptions into explicit checkpoints.

These appear to be the same concept but the naming is inconsistent across sections, which could confuse an implementing agent about whether 'material edit' and 'material event commit' are synonymous or subtly different.\n\nNo outright orphan ACs without any description coverage were found beyond the above.",

For the bug-exemption AC, add a one-sentence description of the implementation mechanism: e.g. 'The gate router checks ticket type; bug-type tickets return a pass-through (no review required) and are logged.' 2. For the experiment-defaults AC, either describe the experiments inline or explicitly state 'experiments are a prerequisite tracked in ticket #X.' 3. Standardize terminology: pick either 'material edit' or 'material event commit' and use it consistently across design summary, success criteria, and ACs — add a one-line definition at first use.",

findings.location: AC list items: 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python, polyglot, fail-open: readiness shape … file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep), package existence (syft + registry + fallback), oversize signals, task-DAG validity, AC/DD quality, destructive sniff.' and 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points.' and 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding … (extends the close-gate signing).'
findings.finding: The plan names functional components and tooling integrations (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard, rebar.llm catalog/contracts/prompt/runner, close-gate signing) but never enumerates the actual files, modules, or packages that will be created or modified. The blast radius — particularly around the close-gate signing extension, the reducer (for the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar skip), the `claim` command, and rebar.llm's orchestrator — is acknowledged only by reference to existing systems but not mapped to concrete file/module paths. Without access to the live codebase it is impossible to confirm whether these integration points exist at the implied locations or whether callers of `claim` / the completion-verifier need updating.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / modules touched' section listing at minimum: (1) new modules to be created (e.g. `rebar/plan_review/layer1.py`, `rebar/plan_review/layer2.py`, `rebar/plan_review/registry.py`, `rebar/plan_review/attestation.py`), (2) existing modules to be modified (e.g. the `claim` command module, the reducer, the close-gate signing module, rebar.llm orchestrator hooks), and (3) any config schema files that gain new keys. Explicitly note that callers of `claim` require no interface change (fast attestation check is backward-compatible) or flag where they do.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Criteria registry: each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner).' and 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep … syft + registry + LLM-fallback … scc + lizard.' and 'Execution: chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB … default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments.'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly states it reuses rebar.llm's existing catalog/contracts/prompt/runner and existing polyglot tooling already in the repo, which is a strong anti-slop signal. However, two areas could represent configuration proliferation or premature abstraction that warrants a closer look without the codebase: (1) The 'criteria registry as declarative descriptors + generic orchestrator' is only used for this one gate today (the completion-verifier is described as separate); if the completion-verifier does not already use this same registry/orchestrator pattern, this is a new abstraction with a single call-site, violating Rule-of-Three. (2) The configurable knobs surface (chunking granularity, per-criterion advisory/blocking, budget cap, INDETERMINATE disposition, escalation model, depth posture) is extensive; without knowing what already exists in rebar's config schema it is impossible to determine whether this is additive proliferation or a natural extension.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify in the plan whether the completion-verifier already uses a criteria-registry/orchestrator pattern that this epic extends (reuse) or whether this epic introduces the abstraction for the first time (new). If new, note at least one planned second consumer (e.g. the bug-review follow-on F6) to satisfy Rule-of-Three rationale. Also list which config keys are net-new vs. extensions of existing keys.

findings.location: Full plan

Multiple sections: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' (AC item 11); 'Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' (Context); 'complexity' routing pivot (Design summary, Trigger/enforcement)
findings.finding: Several design decisions are deferred to external artefacts an executing agent cannot access. (1) The AC states chunking/budget/depth defaults must be 'grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' but no experiment ticket or result summary is referenced in the plan itself — an agent must discover and interpret those results independently. (2) The authoritative design detail is said to live in a session log ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b') that is not embedded or summarised beyond what already appears in the description; if that log contains binding decisions not captured here (e.g. exact Layer-1 blocking thresholds, signing algorithm, sidecar schema), the agent must re-derive them. (3) 'Complexity' is used as a routing pivot throughout ('proportionate-depth routing (complexity + has_children)', 'escalation by size+complexity+centrality') but is never operationally defined — no formula, metric, or range is given, leaving the agent to choose an implementation. (4) 'MATERIAL event commit' invalidation is defined at the concept level ('description/AC/file_impact/decomposition edits invalidate; tags/comments/links/assignee do not') but the concrete event-type list or field names in rebar's event log are not specified, so the agent must infer the mapping.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an inline summary of the experiment conclusions that set the chunking/budget/depth defaults (or link to a readable artefact in the repo). Define 'complexity' with a concrete metric or scoring rule (e.g., scc line count + cyclomatic score bracket). List the exact rebar event types / fields that constitute a 'material edit'. If the session log is the canonical source, extract the binding decisions into an Appendix section in this ticket so the agent can proceed without external lookup.

findings.location: Title: 'Plan-review verification gate (open→in_progress)'; Design summary; Success Criteria; Acceptance Criteria
Acceptance Criteria (all 12 items); Success Criteria (all 6 items); Design summary
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan. None of the Acceptance Criteria or Success Criteria describe how the gate itself will be tested. There is no mention of: unit tests for Layer-1 deterministic checks (e.g., what happens when solidlsp is absent, when ast-grep crashes, when a language is unsupported — the fail-open guarantee); integration tests for the open→in_progress claim path (with and without a valid attestation, with an invalidated attestation, with --force); contract/behavioral tests for the signing + invalidation logic; tests for the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event (schema, reducer-skip, fingerprints); tests verifying the coaching feedback format (grounded/specific/ranked/checklist); regression tests confirming bugs are exempt; and performance/budget-cap tests (INDETERMINATE outcome path). The plan's own coaching spec (Layer-2 AC) says findings must be 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' and 'verification/termination' is a Layer-2 criterion — yet no analog is applied to the plan's own verification strategy. No RED-before-GREEN ordering is indicated for any criterion.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a dedicated '## Testing Plan' section that covers at minimum: (1) Unit tests for each Layer-1 check with a fail-open fixture (no LSP server, unsupported lang → PASS, not FAIL). (2) Integration/end-to-end tests for the claim gate: valid cert → proceeds; absent cert → blocked; stale cert (post-material-edit) → blocked; --force + justification → proceeds + logged. (3) Sidecar event contract test: emit REVIEW_RESULT, verify reducer ignores it, verify fingerprint fields are present. (4) Signing round-trip test: sign → verify → tamper → verify-fails. (5) Coaching-output format tests: per-criterion verdict structure conforms to schema. Specify that Layer-1 fail-open tests run RED first (assert FAIL before adding stub server) to confirm the harness catches real failures.

findings.location: AC item: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).'
findings.finding: This acceptance criterion defers to external experiment results ('see linked experiment work') that are not part of the deliverable described in this ticket. It is unclear whether (a) the experiments are a prerequisite that must be completed and their results available before this work starts, (b) the experiments are a concurrent deliverable, or (c) the agent is expected to run the experiments as part of this session. If the experiment results do not yet exist, this AC is unverifiable within the session. Similarly, AC 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented … anti-slop; plus triggered overlays (router-selected) …' references a broad, open-ended 'bounded broad pass' whose completeness is hard to objectively measure. Additionally, the attestation AC requires a 'signed result'—verifying that the signing scheme is cryptographically sound requires access to the real signing infrastructure, which cannot be fully validated in a single session without live tooling.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (1) specify that the experiment ticket(s) are a blocking prerequisite and reference their ticket IDs, or (2) define the default values inline in this plan (even if provisional) so the AC is verifiable without external dependency. For the 'anti-slop' and 'broad pass' criteria, add a concrete definition or acceptance test so an agent can tell when they are satisfied.

findings.location: Context section; Design summary opening paragraph
Multiple AC items vs. Design summary / Follow-on sections
findings.finding: Three specific coherence concerns: (1) The AC item 'Bugs are exempt from this gate (routed to a follow-on)' says 'routed to a follow-on,' implying active routing logic is in scope, but the Design summary says 'Bugs are SKIPPED in this epic' with no mention of routing infrastructure — the AC language 'routed' may over-commit. (2) The AC item 'Docs: how the gate works …' has no corresponding section in the Design summary describing the documentation deliverable (scope, format, location). A documentation AC with no design description is a minor orphan. (3) Terminology: the plan alternates between 'coaching review' (Design summary) and 'advisory criteria' (AC), and between 'plan-review attestation' (AC) and 'signed attestation'/'cert'/'certification' (Design summary, Success Criteria) — these all appear to refer to the same artifacts, but a reader must infer the mapping. No true duplicates were found.
findings.suggested_edit: For (1): change 'routed to a follow-on' to 'skipped (exempt); dedicated follow-on F6 will address bug-ticket review' to match the design. For (2): add a sentence in the Design summary describing what the documentation covers (location, format, intended audience). For (3): introduce a short glossary or consistently pick one term per concept — e.g., always 'coaching review' for Layer 2 and 'plan-review attestation' for the signed artifact.

findings.location: Success Criteria + Acceptance Criteria sections; Design summary ('Execution', 'Attestation')
findings.finding:
The plan describes a clear overall end-state (signed attestation required at open→in_progress, Layer-1 blocking, Layer-2 advisory, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, CI-checkable cert), and the Acceptance Criteria list is fairly comprehensive. However, two reachability gaps exist that cannot be fully resolved without codebase/experiment access:

1. AC bullet: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).' The experiments are referenced but not in scope — they are a dependency. If the experiment work is not done/filed before this epic closes, this AC cannot be satisfied, and the gate launches without calibrated defaults. The plan provides no fallback position ('use X as a temporary default if experiments are incomplete').

2. The plan states the review runs 'OUT-OF-BAND' and that 'claim is a FAST check for a valid signed attestation.' The handoff/sequencing between the out-of-band review completing and the claim check reading the result is not specified (e.g., polling, event-driven, or synchronous subprocess). This gap could leave a valid execution path where a claim is attempted before the out-of-band review has persisted its attestation, and there is no AC or step that covers this race/ordering concern.

These are not fatal but would cause rework if encountered during implementation.
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Add a fallback AC or note: 'If experiment results are unavailable at implementation time, defaults of [placeholder values] are used and documented as provisional, to be updated once experiments complete.' 2. Add an AC or design note clarifying the ordering contract between the out-of-band review and the claim check (e.g., 'claim check reads the most-recent signed REVIEW_RESULT event from the ticket event log; if none exists, it blocks and prompts the agent to run the review first').

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all 12 bullets); Design summary (all subsections); 'Follow-on work' section
findings.finding:
This is an explicitly labeled epic with no decomposition into child tickets present in the plan itself. The Acceptance Criteria enumerate at least 12 distinct, technically non-trivial deliverables across different subsystems:
- Layer-1 deterministic criteria (LSP/ast-grep, syft, scc/lizard, DAG validity, etc.)
- Layer-2 advisory criteria (10+ distinct judgment checks)
- Criteria registry + generic orchestrator (reusing rebar.llm)
- Attestation signing + material-event-commit binding + invalidation logic
- `claim` integration (fast path check + --force override)
- Configurable chunking + depth routing + escalation + per-plan budget cap + checkpointing + INDETERMINATE outcome
- Feedback-authoring coaching spec conformance
- REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip + override logging
- Per-criterion config (advisory | blocking-with-override)
- Bug exemption routing
- Empirically-grounded defaults (dependent on external experiment tickets)
- Documentation

The plan says 'Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' and lists 10+ follow-on tickets, but does NOT define the child tickets that decompose this epic into executable units. An agent claiming this ticket would have no child structure to navigate — just a large, undivided scope spanning polyglot tooling integration, LLM orchestration, event system changes, signing infrastructure, config/routing logic, and docs. This is far too large to execute coherently in one session.

The 'Follow-on work' section (F1–F12) is correctly deferred, but the epic-level decomposition INTO children (the actual work items) is absent — this is the missing piece for a parent ticket.
findings.suggested_edit:
File and link child tickets before assigning/claiming this epic. A suggested decomposition:
- C1: Layer-1 deterministic criteria implementation (readiness shape, file/symbol resolution, package existence, oversize, DAG validity, AC/DD quality, destructive sniff)
- C2: Layer-2 advisory criteria implementation (all judgment checks + overlays + broad pass)
- C3: Criteria registry + generic orchestrator (reusing rebar.llm extension points)
- C4: Attestation signing, material-event-commit binding, and invalidation logic
- C5: `claim` integration (fast attestation check + --force override + bug exemption routing)
- C6: Execution engine (chunking config, depth routing, escalation, budget cap, checkpointing, INDETERMINATE)
- C7: REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip + override logging + offline-analysis readiness
- C8: Per-criterion config surface (advisory | blocking-with-override) + documentation
- C9: Calibration experiments (prerequisite/blocker for C6 defaults)

Add a parent→children coverage check to the AC: 'Child tickets defined and cover all AC bullets.'

findings.location: Design summary: 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)'; 'Execution'; 'Attestation'; 'Criteria registry'; Context paragraph
findings.finding:
The plan rests on several codebase-state assertions that an executor would need to verify against the live repo before relying on them:

1. **'solidlsp (already in repo)'** — The plan treats solidlsp as an existing, ready-to-use integration. An executor must verify: is solidlsp already wired as a rebar dependency and callable? Is its API surface stable enough to build Layer-1 file/symbol checks on top of without refactoring? Cannot confirm without codebase access.

2. **'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'** — The plan assumes rebar.llm exposes specific extension points (catalog, contracts, prompt, runner) that the criteria registry and orchestrator can plug into. An executor must verify these extension points exist and are designed for reuse at the required abstraction level.

3. **'extends the close-gate signing'** — The attestation design assumes the close-gate signing mechanism is factored as a reusable library, not a one-off implementation. An executor must verify this before designing the plan-review signing on top of it.

4. **'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time'** — The plan asserts this computation is available at plan time to drive model escalation. An executor must verify whether a centrality/blast-radius signal is already produced during ticket creation/planning or needs to be built as part of this epic.

5. **'reducer-IGNORED SIDECAR'** — The plan assumes the event reducer has a skip-by-type mechanism (or that adding one is trivial). An executor must verify whether the reducer already supports event-type filtering before committing to this as a 'cheap, in-scope' approach.

None of these are asserted to be wrong — all are plausible given the described system — but each is a load-bearing assumption that, if incorrect, would require design changes.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief 'Prerequisites / assumptions to verify' subsection in the plan (or as a pre-implementation task in the first child ticket) listing these five assumptions with a specific verification step for each. Example: 'solidlsp already in repo → verify: import rebar.lsp.solidlsp works and the file/symbol query interface is stable (see module X).' This costs little and prevents mid-implementation surprises.

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' and AC: 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented: ... intent fidelity (2-step blind-restate) ...'
findings.finding: Two acceptance criteria depend on external artifacts that are not deliverable within a single session: (1) 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' explicitly defers to a linked experiment work item that is not described here and may not yet exist as completable work — it is unclear whether the experiments are already done or must be run as part of this epic. If they must be run, the AC is not completable in one pass. (2) 'intent fidelity (2-step blind-restate)' is described only by label; the verification method for whether this criterion is correctly implemented is unclear and potentially subjective. Additionally, AC 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings … that measurably help an agent revise the plan' (SC #3) uses the word 'measurably' without specifying how measurement occurs — the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar is explicitly deferred to follow-on (F1 in follow-ons) for actual analysis, so within-epic verification of 'measurably helps' is not possible.
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Clarify whether the chunking/budget/depth experiments are pre-existing results (cite them or inline their conclusions) or whether running them is in scope — if in scope, scope them explicitly as a subtask. 2. Replace the SC-3 word 'measurably' with a concrete proxy that is checkable in-session (e.g., 'coaching findings are grounded, specific, severity-ranked, and checklist-form as defined in the coaching spec'). 3. Add a brief description of the 2-step blind-restate method so its correct implementation is verifiable.

findings.location: Context section: '~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects' and 'a BROAD, DIVERSE CLIENT BASE (many languages, frameworks, project structures, and ticket-creation styles)'; Design summary: 'posture is to COACH agents to author better plans'

Multiple locations: SC #3 vs AC 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec'; Design summary 'Routing pivots on has_children + complexity' vs AC 'proportionate-depth routing (complexity + has_children); escalation by size+complexity+centrality'; AC 'Criteria registry … reusing rebar.llm extension points' vs Design summary 'generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)'
findings.finding: Three potential coherence issues: (1) The Success Criteria list 6 high-level items, but AC item 'Bugs are exempt from this gate' has no corresponding SC entry — it is described in the design ('Bugs are SKIPPED in this epic') but not surfaced in the SC list, making it an orphan AC. (2) SC #3 says 'coaching findings … that measurably help an agent revise the plan' while the AC says 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec (grounded/specific/ranked/affirming/checklist)' — these are near-duplicates but worded differently enough to invite confusion about which is the authoritative definition. (3) The design summary says routing pivots on 'has_children + complexity' but the AC adds a third dimension 'centrality' to escalation ('escalation by size+complexity+centrality') — this third dimension is mentioned in the design summary under execution but the routing description omits it, creating a minor inconsistency. None of these are blocking, but they could cause an agent to under-implement the bugs-exempt behavior or conflate the SC and AC coaching requirements.
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Add a SC entry such as 'Bug-type tickets are identified and exempted from this gate at claim time' to match the AC. 2. Consolidate the coaching-spec wording: either reference the AC coaching spec from SC #3 (e.g., 'as defined by the coaching spec in AC') or remove the vague 'measurably' qualifier from the SC. 3. Align the design summary routing paragraph to mention centrality/blast-radius alongside complexity and has_children so the three dimensions appear consistently everywhere.

findings.location: Success Criteria + Acceptance Criteria sections overall; specifically ACs: 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding...', 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check...', 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding:
The plan has a reasonably complete end-state picture for the gate mechanism itself, but two termination/verification gaps remain:

1. **Experiment dependency**: AC bullet 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' defers a concrete, measurable completion signal to externally-linked experiment work that is not described here. The executor has no clear stopping criterion for when the experiments are 'done enough' to set the defaults — this sub-item has an open-ended completion condition.

2. **Layer-2 quality verification**: Success Criterion 3 says findings must 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' but no measurement or acceptance test for that quality property is described anywhere in the plan. This is effectively an unverifiable success criterion at delivery time unless a concrete test (e.g., a held-out set of plans + human/automated rating) is specified or explicitly deferred to F10 (calibrated abstention + labeled gold set). It is plausible F10 is intentionally the home for this — but the plan does not say so explicitly, leaving the gap ambiguous.
findings.suggested_edit: For the experiment dependency: add an explicit deliverable such as 'experiment results doc (or linked artifact) specifying the chosen chunk-size and budget-cap defaults is present at ticket close'. For the Layer-2 measurability gap: either (a) add a concrete proxy acceptance test (e.g., 'coaching findings are reviewed by one human against the coaching-spec checklist on N sampled plans before merge') or (b) explicitly note that end-to-end quality measurement is deferred to F10 and remove 'measurably' from Success Criterion 3.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — full list of 12 bullet points; Design summary — 'Two layers', 'Criteria registry', 'Attestation', 'claim integration', 'Configurable chunking...', 'REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event', 'Docs'
findings.finding:
This is an epic-level ticket, and it is explicitly structured as one (the description calls it 'this epic'). The plan's own follow-on section (F1–F12) acknowledges significant downstream work, but the epic itself has NO child ticket decomposition described or referenced — only a vague note that 'full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b'. The 12 AC bullets span at least 7–8 independently buildable subsystems:
- Layer-1 deterministic criteria (multi-tool, polyglot)
- Layer-2 advisory criteria (11+ distinct criteria)
- Criteria registry + generic orchestrator
- Attestation + signing + invalidation
- `claim` integration
- Configurable chunking + routing + escalation + budget-cap + checkpointing
- `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event + reducer changes
- Docs

Each of these is a non-trivial implementation unit. Executing all of them in a single session is not feasible; the plan does not describe how the epic is decomposed into child tickets, which child tickets exist or should be created, or how ordering/dependencies between children are managed. For an epic, the absence of a child decomposition is a direct gap against criterion G5.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a child-ticket breakdown section listing one child ticket per major subsystem (e.g., Layer-1 tooling, Layer-2 criteria, registry/orchestrator, attestation/signing, claim integration, chunking/routing/budget, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, docs+config). Each child should be small enough for one session. Specify the dependency ordering (e.g., registry/orchestrator before Layer-2 criteria; attestation before claim integration). This decomposition can live as a 'Child tickets' section in the epic description.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep (resolution/callers); syft + registry + LLM-fallback (deps); scc + lizard (size)'; also 'Criteria registry: each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)'
findings.finding:
The plan rests on several specific claims about the existing codebase that cannot be verified from the plan text alone:

1. **'solidlsp (already in repo)'** — the plan assumes solidlsp is present and usable as an LSP harness. If it is not actually integrated (or is a stub/experiment), Layer-1 file/symbol resolution cannot be built as described without additional setup work.

2. **'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'** — the plan assumes rebar.llm already exposes these specific extension points (catalog, contracts, prompt, runner) in a form that a criteria registry + generic orchestrator can plug into. If these abstractions don't exist or have a different shape, the orchestrator design may need significant revision.

3. **'extends the close-gate signing'** (Attestation AC) — the plan assumes the close-gate signing mechanism is already in a reusable, extensible form. If it is tightly coupled to close-gate specifics, extension may require refactoring that is not scoped here.

4. **'reducer skips the type'** (REVIEW_RESULT sidecar) — assumes the reducer is already architected in a way where adding a skip-by-event-type rule is a minor change, not a structural one.

These are all reasonable assumptions for a mature codebase, but each one should be verified against the actual repo before an executor designs child tickets around them.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Prerequisite verification' section (or annotate the relevant AC bullets) naming each assumption and the specific file/module/test an executor should inspect to confirm it before starting implementation. For example: 'Confirm solidlsp integration at <path>; confirm rebar.llm exposes catalog/contracts/prompt/runner at <module>; confirm close-gate signing is parameterized at <module>; confirm reducer event-type dispatch at <path>.'

findings.location: AC: 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python, polyglot, fail-open: readiness shape, file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep), package existence (syft + registry + fallback), oversize signals, task-DAG validity, AC/DD quality, destructive sniff' and 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding + invalidation-on-material-edit + coverage record (extends the close-gate signing)'
findings.finding: The plan names several tooling integrations (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, lizard, scc) and explicitly extends the close-gate signing mechanism, but does not name any specific source files, modules, or symbols in the rebar codebase that will be touched or created. Consumers of the current `claim` command path, the existing completion-verifier, and the `rebar.llm` extension points are referenced by description ('reusing rebar.llm extension points', 'extends the close-gate signing') but without module/file paths. It is therefore impossible to confirm from the plan text alone whether all callers of the open→in_progress transition are covered, or whether the attestation storage schema change has downstream readers that need updating. The blast radius is partially acknowledged ('centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time') but only as a runtime concept, not as a design-time enumeration of what this epic itself touches.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a section (even brief) listing the key existing modules/files expected to be modified — e.g. the claim handler, the close-gate signing module being extended, the rebar.llm orchestrator entry point, the event reducer (for the REVIEW_RESULT skip) — and note which of those have other callers/consumers that must stay compatible. This turns the AMBIGUOUS blast-radius into an acknowledged one.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points' and 'a per-criterion config knob lets a project make a judgment criterion BLOCKING' and 'Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing; escalation by size+complexity+centrality; per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing'
findings.finding: The plan deliberately reuses rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner and extends the close-gate signing mechanism — these are explicit anti-NIH choices, which is good. However, the configuration surface introduced is significant: per-criterion advisory/blocking toggle, chunking knob, budget cap, depth routing, escalation model, INDETERMINATE blocking toggle, and project risk-posture — six or more independent knobs. Without visibility into what config infrastructure already exists in rebar, it is not possible to confirm whether this proliferates a new config layer or extends an existing one. The Rule-of-Three concern is mild: the 'bounded broad open-ended pass' (Layer-2 final step) appears to be a single-use construct with no shared abstraction, but the plan frames it as part of the same criteria-registry pattern, so it is plausibly justified. The main AMBIGUOUS risk is whether the new config surface duplicates something already present.
findings.suggested_edit: Explicitly state which existing rebar config mechanism (file, schema, namespace) the new knobs will extend, and confirm no new top-level config namespace is introduced solely for this feature. If a new namespace is genuinely needed, briefly justify why the existing one cannot accommodate it.

findings.location: Whole plan
Design summary – 'Execution' bullet; AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: Several design decisions are deferred to linked artefacts that are not reproduced in the plan. Specifically: (1) 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' for chunking — the executor must read the linked session log to know what defaults to ship; (2) 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' — no algorithm or data-source is named; (3) 'escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality' — the escalation thresholds are undefined; (4) 'experiment results (see linked experiment work)' in AC is a forward reference to work not yet described in the ticket. These are not fully blocking because the plan instructs the executor to ground defaults in experiments, but the experiments themselves are not described here, so an executor without access to session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b cannot proceed on those items without asking a clarifying question.
findings.suggested_edit: Inline at least the key empirically-derived defaults (e.g., 'default chunk size = N criteria per call; per-plan token budget cap = X') and the centrality/blast-radius algorithm sketch (e.g., 'use the existing rebar dependency graph, count unique transitive consumers as the blast-radius proxy') directly in the AC or in a 'Defaults' sub-section, so the executor is not gated on reading the session log.

findings.location: Title, Context, Design summary, Success Criteria, Acceptance Criteria
Acceptance Criteria (all twelve bullet points); Success Criteria (all six bullet points)
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan at any level of specificity. There is no mention of unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, or any verification approach for the new gate. Concretely missing: (1) How is Layer-1 (deterministic checks) tested — e.g., are there fixture tickets with known structural flaws that must be detected? (2) How is the fail-open/polyglot behavior verified — e.g., what test confirms that an unsupported language → PASS rather than FAIL? (3) How is the attestation signing + invalidation-on-material-edit tested? (4) How is the `claim` integration tested — e.g., does a test assert the claim is blocked when no valid attestation exists? (5) How is the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event tested — e.g., does the reducer correctly skip it? (6) How are Layer-2 advisory findings tested for coaching-spec conformance (grounded, severity-ranked, checklist-form)? The Follow-on section lists F10 (calibrated abstention + labeled gold set) but that is deferred; there is no in-scope testing work described at all.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Testing approach' section (or expand the AC) covering at minimum: (a) fixture-driven unit tests for each Layer-1 criterion with both triggering and fail-open cases; (b) an integration test for the claim→gate→attestation flow for open→in_progress; (c) a reducer test asserting REVIEW_RESULT events are ignored; (d) a contract/snapshot test for the attestation schema + invalidation logic; (e) a test for the --force override path recording the justification. Red-before-green ordering should be stated where the feature is greenfield.

findings.location: Multiple sections: 'Execution' bullet ('default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'), AC item 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)', and Design summary 'Routing pivots on has_children + complexity'
findings.finding: Several design decisions are deferred to external artefacts or future experiments that an executing agent cannot access from this plan alone: (1) Chunking/budget/depth defaults are explicitly deferred to 'experiment results' referenced only by a session-log ID ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b') that an agent may not be able to retrieve. If those experiments haven't been run yet, or the session log is unavailable, the agent has no concrete starting values to implement. (2) The complexity threshold that drives routing ('complexity + has_children') is never defined — what unit/scale/threshold constitutes 'high complexity'? (3) 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' is described as an input to escalation, but no specification is given for how centrality is computed (call-graph depth? file fan-out? something else?). (4) The 'bounded broad open-ended pass' in Layer-2 is mentioned but its scope, prompt shape, and output contract are not described. These are not fatal blockers for design work, but an agent asked to implement concrete code would need to make arbitrary choices or stop to ask.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Provisional defaults' subsection that gives concrete starting values (e.g. 'default chunk size = N criteria per call; default budget = X tokens per plan; complexity threshold = lizard CCN ≥ Y or file count ≥ Z; centrality = transitive file-impact count'). Flag these as 'to be tuned by experiments' so the intent is clear, but give the agent a concrete fallback. Describe the broad-pass in one sentence of output contract.

findings.location: Title ('Plan-review verification gate (open→in_progress)'), Design summary, Success Criteria, Acceptance Criteria
Acceptance Criteria (all 12 bullet items) and Success Criteria (all 6 items) — no mention of testing approach anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan contains zero description of how the implemented gate will be tested. There is no mention of: unit tests for Layer-1 deterministic checks (e.g. fail-open behavior, each structural criterion); integration tests for the open→in_progress claim path (presence/absence/staleness of attestation); contract tests for the criteria registry and orchestrator; tests for the signing/invalidation logic; tests for the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event being reducer-ignored; failure-path coverage (tool crashes, timeouts, unsupported languages); or RED-before-GREEN ordering. For a feature that is itself a verification gate — and one whose core selling point is coaching agents toward rigor — the absence of any stated testing plan is a significant gap and will likely cause rework when the implementation is reviewed.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Testing approach' section (or an AC item) covering at minimum: (1) Unit tests for each Layer-1 criterion with at least one PASS, one FAIL, and one fail-open (unsupported lang / tool crash) case. (2) Integration test: mock a ticket event stream, assert that `claim` is blocked without an attestation and succeeds with a valid one. (3) Staleness test: emit a material-edit event after signing, assert the attestation is invalidated. (4) Layer-2 coaching output shape test: assert that findings returned meet the grounded/specific/severity-ranked contract. (5) Sidecar event test: assert reducer skips REVIEW_RESULT type without error. Tests should be RED before the implementation and GREEN after.

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' and 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented: ... intent fidelity (2-step blind-restate)...'
findings.finding: Two ACs are potentially out-of-session or depend on external artefacts: (1) 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' defers to 'linked experiment work' that is not part of this epic — if those experiments haven't been run yet, an agent cannot derive the defaults within this session. (2) 'intent fidelity (2-step blind-restate)' is described as a Layer-2 criterion but what constitutes a passing vs. failing restate is not operationally defined; measurability depends on a rubric not present in the plan. Additionally, 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings that measurably help an agent revise the plan' (Success Criterion 3) embeds a real-world outcome ('measurably help') that cannot be verified in-session — only the structural conformance of the output (grounded/ranked/checklist) can be checked in-session.
findings.suggested_edit: For the experiment-results AC, either (a) treat the experiments as a prerequisite and reference their committed outputs, or (b) restate as 'defaults are set to X pending experiment results; experiments are a follow-on'. For 'measurably help', rephrase the success criterion to the in-session proxy: 'Each advisory finding is grounded in a criterion, names a file/section, carries a severity level, and includes a suggested edit when confidence is high.'

findings.location: Context section: 'when an agent claims a ticket (open→in_progress), nothing checks that the ticket's implementation plan is sound before the agent executes it… ~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects.' and 'It is a rebar FEATURE used by a BROAD, DIVERSE CLIENT BASE'
Multiple sections: Success Criteria vs. Acceptance Criteria; 'REVIEW_RESULT' sidecar; 'centrality/blast-radius'; 'INDETERMINATE'
findings.finding: Three potential coherence/coverage gaps worth flagging: (1) **Orphan Success Criterion vs. AC**: Success Criterion 6 ('The signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process') does not have a clearly corresponding AC. The closest is 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding…' but it says nothing about the external-CI-check interface. The 'Docs' AC mentions 'the CI-check pattern' but docs alone do not cover the checkable-by-CI requirement. (2) **'centrality/blast-radius'** is mentioned in the Design Summary ('centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time') and in the AC ('escalation by size+complexity+centrality'), but neither the Design Summary nor any AC describes *how* centrality is computed or what data source feeds it — this could be an implied dependency on undescribed tooling. (3) **Terminology**: The plan uses both 'open-ended pass' (Design Summary) and 'bounded broad pass' (AC Layer-2 list) for what appears to be the same concept; a reader could treat these as distinct passes. These are minor and unlikely to block execution, but worth clarifying.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC or note under the Attestation AC explicitly covering the external CI-check interface (e.g., a CLI flag or HTTP endpoint). Align 'open-ended pass' / 'bounded broad pass' to a single term throughout. Add a brief note on the centrality/blast-radius computation source (e.g., 'derived from the dependency graph already computed by solidlsp').

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; Follow-on: 'Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b'
findings.finding: Several concrete numerical/configuration decisions are deferred to external artefacts an executing agent may not be able to resolve independently. Specifically: (1) chunking/budget/depth defaults are said to be 'grounded in the experiment results' but the experiments are referenced as linked work rather than summarised here — if that linked work hasn't been completed or is not machine-accessible, the agent must make up the numbers; (2) the full design detail 'lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' — if that log is not reachable at execution time the plan is partly underdetermined; (3) 'escalation to a stronger model by size+complexity+centrality' does not specify the threshold values or the model ladder, leaving a real design decision to the executor. None of these individually blocks execution of most of the feature, but collectively they require the agent to either stop and ask or make unverified numeric choices.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a small 'Default values' sub-section that either (a) reproduces the key numeric outputs of the experiments inline (chunk size, budget cap, depth thresholds, model escalation ladder), or (b) explicitly marks them as TBD-experiment and notes that the executor should run the experiment ticket first, treating those AC as blocked until results are available. Replace the session-log reference with an inline design appendix or a link to a committed document.
