findings.location: Title: 'Plan-review verification gate (open→in_progress)'; full plan body
Acceptance Criteria section (all 12 ACs); Success Criteria section; Design summary
findings.finding: The plan specifies what to build in considerable detail but contains no testing plan whatsoever. There is no mention of: (1) how the Layer-1 deterministic checks will be tested (unit tests per check? integration fixtures?); (2) how the Layer-2 advisory criteria will be evaluated for correctness (golden-plan fixtures, prompt regression tests?); (3) how the attestation signing/invalidation logic will be verified (boundary cases: material vs. non-material edits); (4) how the `claim` integration will be tested end-to-end; (5) failure-path coverage for the fail-open polyglot tooling (e.g., LSP crash → PASS, not FAIL); (6) any RED-before-GREEN ordering or TDD intent; (7) any mention of the experiment work that is supposed to ground defaults — without tests those defaults cannot be validated. For a feature with explicit blocking-gate behaviour and a signing/security-signal component, the absence of even a high-level testing strategy is a significant gap that will force rework when reviewers or CI ask for coverage.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Testing plan' section (or sub-bullets under each AC) covering at minimum: (a) unit tests for each Layer-1 check with at least one pass, one fail, and one fail-open (unsupported tool) fixture per check; (b) contract/snapshot tests for the attestation schema and signing round-trip; (c) integration test for the claim gate (valid cert → allowed, missing cert → blocked, stale cert → blocked, --force → override recorded); (d) prompt-regression / golden-output tests for at least two Layer-2 criteria; (e) a budget/chunking smoke test confirming INDETERMINATE is returned when budget is exhausted. Explicitly note RED-before-GREEN for the deterministic Layer-1 checks.

findings.location: Design summary – 'Polyglot tooling' bullet; AC row 1 ('file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep)'); AC row 6 ('Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding … extends the close-gate signing')
findings.finding: The plan names several implementation targets at a conceptual level (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, lizard, scc, rebar.llm extension points, the existing completion-verifier signing path, the `claim` command, the reducer, the REVIEW_RESULT event type) but never lists specific file paths, module names, or symbol names in the rebar codebase that will be created or modified. Without access to the live repo it is impossible to confirm (a) that the named tools are already present and importable where the plan assumes, (b) that the 'claim' command has the hook points described, (c) that the close-gate signing infrastructure is actually reusable as stated ('extends the close-gate signing'), or (d) what existing callers/consumers of the open→in_progress transition would need updating (e.g. tests, CI scripts, other commands that invoke claim). The blast-radius of touching the open→in_progress gate is acknowledged in the design ('fires at open→in_progress', '--force override') but the concrete consumer list is absent. This is not necessarily wrong — it may all exist as implied — but it cannot be verified from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / modules touched' table listing at minimum: the existing claim-command file, the close-gate signing module being extended, the reducer file (to add REVIEW_RESULT skip), and the config-surface location. Also add a one-sentence blast-radius note naming any other rebar commands or tests that invoke the open→in_progress transition and will need updating.

findings.location: Design summary – 'Criteria registry' bullet ('a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)'); 'Two layers' bullet; 'Attestation' bullet ('extends the close-gate signing')
findings.finding: The plan explicitly claims reuse of rebar.llm's existing catalog/contracts/prompt/runner for the orchestrator, and reuse of the close-gate signing path for attestation — both are strong anti-NIH signals. The configuration surface is potentially large (chunking, budget caps, per-criterion blocking, depth routing, escalation model, INDETERMINATE behaviour, fail-open flags) but the plan acknowledges this as intentional and project-configurable with 'sensible defaults shipped', which is consistent with a feature serving a broad client base. Whether the config surface is gratuitously over-engineered versus genuinely necessary cannot be judged without seeing what already exists in rebar's config layer. One potential Rule-of-Three concern: the 'bounded broad open-ended pass' in Layer 2 (a single extra LLM call beyond the per-criterion pass) is described as a single call-site construct; if the orchestrator already supports per-criterion prompts, adding a bespoke broad-pass runner could be a one-off abstraction — but the plan treats it as a named criterion slot in the same registry, which is a benign reading. No clear NIH or gratuitous abstraction is detectable from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: If the 'bounded broad open-ended pass' uses a materially different execution path from the per-criterion criteria, note explicitly whether it slots into the same orchestrator or requires a separate runner, to pre-empt a one-off abstraction.

findings.location: Multiple: 'Trigger/enforcement' bullet ('A REVIEW PROCESS, not a dialog — the client revises and re-runs'); 'Execution' bullet ('Generous initial budget + depth posture'); AC row 6 ('Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'); Follow-on F10 ('calibrated abstention + labeled gold set')
findings.finding: One potential tension exists between sections: the Design summary says 'Generous initial budget + depth posture to gather data' and AC row 6 says 'Defaults … grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' — but the Follow-on list includes F10 'calibrated abstention + labeled gold set', implying the calibration data does not yet exist. If the experiment results are not complete before this epic ships, the AC requirement for empirically-grounded defaults cannot be met, making it internally inconsistent. Under a benign reading the experiments are a separate linked work item that will complete before this epic's defaults are set; but the plan does not make this sequencing dependency explicit, leaving the AC potentially unverifiable at delivery. A second, smaller tension: the design says 'AMBIGUOUS escalates to a higher-tier adjudicator' but the AC and Follow-on sections never describe what that adjudicator is (human? stronger model? another rebar tool), and no AC row covers it — the design introduces a concept that is neither implemented nor deferred with an explicit follow-on ticket.
findings.suggested_edit: 1) Add a sequencing note in the AC or Context confirming that the experiment results pre-exist or are a prerequisite deliverable, so the empirically-grounded-defaults AC is verifiable at delivery. 2) Either add an AC row for the AMBIGUOUS-escalation adjudicator mechanism, or explicitly name it in the Follow-on list as a deferred item.

findings.location: Success Criteria + Acceptance Criteria sections
findings.finding: The plan's end-state is richly described at the feature level, but there is a gap between 'what we'll do' and 'what done looks like' in two specific areas: (1) The AC references 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' — but the experiment work is not defined in this plan, nor is there an AC that the experiments have been completed and their results captured. If experiments are a prerequisite, there is no termination condition for that work within this ticket. (2) The AC 'Docs: how the gate works...' is listed but there is no criterion specifying what constitutes minimally acceptable documentation (e.g., which doc format, where it lives, who reviews it). These gaps mean an executor cannot self-verify completeness on those items. The core gate logic (Layer-1, Layer-2, attestation, claim integration, sidecar event) does have concrete, checkable ACs, so the majority of the plan is reachable.
findings.suggested_edit: For the experiments dependency: either (a) add a child ticket for the experiment work and reference its completion as a precondition here, or (b) add an AC stating 'Empirical experiment results are on record and referenced in config defaults (linked ticket or session log)' so an executor can verify the dependency is resolved before closing. For docs: add a minimal spec such as 'Docs live in docs/plan-review-gate.md and cover: gate mechanics, config surface, fail-open behavior, cert semantics, CI-check pattern; reviewed by at least one non-author.'

findings.location: Plan description (Design summary, all subsections) + Acceptance Criteria (12 distinct AC items)
findings.finding: This ticket is labelled an 'epic' but is structured as a single executable unit with 12 acceptance criteria spanning at least 7–8 distinct, independently-testable subsystems: (1) Layer-1 deterministic criteria engine with polyglot tooling (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard), (2) Layer-2 LLM advisory criteria engine with ~13 named criteria + overlays + broad pass, (3) Criteria registry + generic orchestrator, (4) Attestation + signing + material-event binding + invalidation, (5) `claim` integration (fast path), (6) Configurable chunking/routing/escalation/budget/checkpointing, (7) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip + override logging, (8) Per-criterion config knob (advisory/blocking), (9) Documentation. Each of these is a meaningful engineering effort. The plan acknowledges 12 follow-on tickets but does not decompose the epic itself into child tickets — the AC and Design summary describe them as a monolithic deliverable. Even treating this as an epic planning document, there are no children tickets referenced or sketched, which means an agent 'claiming' this ticket would attempt to execute all 9 subsystems in a single session, a scope that far exceeds any coherent one-session unit of work and exactly the failure mode the gate itself is designed to prevent.
findings.suggested_edit: Break this epic into at least the following child tickets, each independently claimable and closeable: C1 Criteria registry + generic orchestrator scaffolding; C2 Layer-1 deterministic floor (per-tool: LSP/ast-grep, syft, scc/lizard, shape checks); C3 Layer-2 advisory criteria engine (per-criterion group); C4 Attestation + signing + material-event binding; C5 `claim` fast-path integration; C6 Routing/chunking/escalation/budget/checkpointing config; C7 REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip; C8 Per-criterion blocking config knob; C9 Documentation. Reference these children in the epic description and mark this ticket as a container/parent so the claim gate (which this very epic is building) routes it through container-coverage checks.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)' subsection; also 'Execution' subsection
findings.finding: The plan makes several assertions about the existing codebase that an executor would need to verify before relying on them: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' — the plan assumes solidlsp is present and functional in the rebar repo; if it is absent, partially integrated, or API-incompatible, the Layer-1 file/symbol/import-resolution criterion cannot be implemented as designed without additional work. (2) 'reusing rebar.llm extension points' — the plan asserts rebar.llm already has a catalog/contracts/prompt/runner pattern that the criteria registry and orchestrator can plug into; if those extension points don't exist or have a different interface, significant re-design would be required. (3) 'extends the close-gate signing' — attestation is described as an extension of an existing close-gate signing mechanism; if that mechanism's API is not actually extensible in the assumed way, the attestation design would need rework. (4) 'reducer-ignored SIDECAR' — the plan assumes the reducer can be cheaply taught to skip a new event type; this depends on the reducer's current architecture. These are all verifiable against the live codebase, which is not available for this review.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Verified prerequisites' section (or annotate the relevant AC) listing: (a) solidlsp integration status + the API surface the plan relies on; (b) the specific rebar.llm extension points that will be reused (interface names/signatures); (c) the close-gate signing API and how attestation will extend it; (d) the reducer's current event-type dispatch mechanism. Treat each as a spike/investigation task at the start of the relevant child ticket so assumptions are validated before design is locked.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)' bullet; AC: 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python, polyglot, fail-open: … file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep)'
findings.finding: The plan names the primary tooling entry-points (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard, rebar.llm catalog/contracts/prompt/runner) but does not enumerate the specific rebar source files/modules that will be created or modified. For a feature of this scope, the blast radius is non-trivial: at minimum the `claim` command/handler, the event reducer (to skip REVIEW_RESULT), the attestation/signing subsystem (shared with the close gate), and the criteria-registry + orchestrator are all new or mutated touch-points. Whether existing callers of `claim`, the existing completion-verifier, the event reducer, or CI integration helpers also need updating cannot be confirmed from the plan text alone — those relationships require inspecting the live codebase. The plan does acknowledge the blast-radius conceptually ('extends the close-gate signing', 'reducer-IGNORED SIDECAR') but stops short of listing affected modules/symbols by name. This is a scope-accuracy gap that could cause the executing agent to miss required edits in already-wired paths.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / modules affected' subsection that lists, at minimum: (1) the claim handler file and the precise check to be inserted, (2) the event-reducer file and the REVIEW_RESULT skip rule, (3) the attestation module being extended, (4) the new criteria-registry + orchestrator modules to be created, and (5) any config-schema files that gain new keys. Mark items whose existence is uncertain as 'TBD — verify in codebase' so the agent knows to confirm rather than assume.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Criteria registry: each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)'; AC: 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points.'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly invokes the Rule-of-Three concern by instructing the agent to 'reuse rebar.llm extension points' — so NIH is acknowledged and guarded against. However, whether the existing catalog/contracts/prompt/runner abstraction is already genuinely generic (i.e., designed for multi-criterion orchestration and not just a single-use completion-verifier pipeline) cannot be confirmed from the plan text alone. If it was built for a single call-site, adding the plan-review orchestrator as a second consumer would satisfy the Rule-of-Three threshold, but the executing agent might still need to refactor it first, which is unacknowledged scope. No other clear over-engineering concern is present: the configuration surface is justified by the stated polyglot/multi-project requirements, and the two-layer architecture is a deliberate design choice with explicit rationale, not gratuitous abstraction.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a note clarifying whether rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner currently supports multi-criterion batching natively or will require a targeted extension, and scope that extension explicitly (e.g., 'extend runner to accept a list of criterion descriptors and return per-criterion verdicts'). This prevents the agent from either silently duplicating logic or over-refactoring shared infrastructure.

findings.location: Multiple sections: 'Trigger / enforcement' bullet vs. AC bullet 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; 'Execution' bullet vs. AC 'Configurable chunking … per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing'
findings.finding:
Two potential internal tensions are present, though neither is a hard contradiction:

1. **Experiment dependency vs. in-scope deliverable:** The plan says 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' (Design summary, Execution bullet) and AC requires 'Defaults … grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).' The linked experiment work is referenced via a session-log ID but is not filed as a prerequisite ticket or a blocking dependency. If those experiments have not yet produced results, the AC requiring empirically-grounded defaults cannot be satisfied within this epic, creating a hidden external dependency that could leave the AC unverifiable at close. The plan does not resolve whether the experiments are already complete, in-flight, or to be run as part of this epic.

2. **Out-of-band review vs. claim-time fast check:** The plan states 'FAST check for a valid signed attestation; the heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND' in Trigger/enforcement, which is consistent throughout (the attestation must be pre-generated before claim). This is coherent as written. No contradiction here.

3. **Advisory-by-default vs. blocking AC language:** The AC states 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria … BLOCKS' and 'judgment criteria are advisory by default' — consistent with Design summary. No contradiction.

The only genuine coherence risk is item 1: the experiment-result dependency is unresolved scope that could prevent the AC from being closed.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a note to the AC or Follow-on section clarifying the status of the experiment work: either (a) the experiments are already complete and results are available at the session-log reference, or (b) running the experiments is explicitly in-scope for this epic as a prerequisite sub-task, or (c) the epic ships with placeholder/conservative defaults and a follow-on ticket upgrades them once experiments conclude. This removes the ambiguity and prevents a close-gate dispute.

findings.location: Success Criteria + Acceptance Criteria sections; Design summary ('Execution' and 'Attestation' bullets)
findings.finding:
The plan describes what the gate must do at a high level and lists well-structured acceptance criteria, but several 'done' signals are underspecified or depend on external artefacts the plan cannot resolve on its own:

1. **Experiment-grounded defaults**: AC item 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' defers the definition of done to experiments that are not described in this plan. It is unclear whether those experiments are in-scope, pre-completed, or filed separately — so the termination condition for this specific AC item cannot be confirmed from plan text alone.

2. **'Measurably help' success criterion**: SC3 states Layer-2 findings must 'measurably help an agent revise the plan', but no measurement mechanism or threshold is defined within this epic (FP/remediation-rate tracking is explicitly deferred to follow-on F1). This makes the SC unverifiable within the scope of this ticket.

3. **Session log reference**: 'Full design detail lives in session log `63cc-ec94-4792-464b`'. If any acceptance criterion or termination condition is only defined there and not in this plan, the plan is incomplete by itself. This cannot be confirmed without access to that log.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. Either inline the experiment-derived defaults (or a placeholder range) directly in this plan, or file the experiment work as an explicit prerequisite ticket and mark this AC as blocked on it.
2. Replace 'measurably help' in SC3 with a concrete proxy (e.g., 'agents who receive a Layer-2 finding report a plan revision within one session, measured by a follow-on REVIEW_RESULT edit event') — or explicitly note it is a qualitative standard validated manually at epic close.
3. Add a brief statement that all normative design decisions referenced in the session log are fully captured in this plan description, so the session log is supplementary only.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all 12 AC items); Follow-on work section; Design summary (all bullets)
findings.finding:
This is an epic-level ticket with no child tickets defined within the plan itself, and its scope is extremely broad — covering at minimum:
- Layer-1 deterministic criteria (8+ distinct checks across polyglot tooling: LSP, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard)
- Layer-2 advisory criteria (11+ distinct LLM-judgment checks + triggered overlays + a broad pass)
- A criteria registry + generic orchestrator
- Attestation signing + material-event-commit binding + invalidation logic
- `claim` command integration
- Configurable chunking, routing, escalation, and budget-cap logic
- `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event + reducer skip + override logging
- Per-criterion config system
- Experiment work to ground defaults
- Documentation

While the plan explicitly states it is an epic and defers F1–F12 to follow-on tickets, it does NOT decompose the epic body itself into child tickets. An autonomous agent picking up this ticket would face an enormous, underdecomposed scope in a single 'unit of work'. The plan references decomposition principles (complexity + has_children routing) but does not apply them to itself.

Specifically: the 12 AC items span at least 5–7 independently deliverable vertical slices (e.g., Layer-1 tooling, Layer-2 LLM review, attestation subsystem, claim integration, observability/sidecar, config surface, docs+experiments), each of which is a multi-session implementation effort. No child ticket structure, ordering, or breakdown is provided.
findings.suggested_edit:
Decompose this epic into explicit child tickets, at minimum along these vertical slices (each should be independently completable and testable):
1. Layer-1 deterministic criteria + polyglot tooling integration
2. Layer-2 advisory criteria + criteria registry + orchestrator
3. Attestation subsystem (signing, material-event-commit binding, invalidation)
4. `claim` integration (fast attestation check + --force override)
5. Configurable chunking / routing / escalation / budget-cap
6. REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip + override logging
7. Experiment work to ground defaults (or mark as prerequisite)
8. Docs

List these as child tickets in this plan and clarify the epic's own AC items map to those children. The epic-level AC items can remain as the definition of done for the overall epic.

findings.location: Design summary bullets: 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)', 'Execution', 'Attestation'; Acceptance Criteria: Layer-1 AC item
findings.finding:
The plan makes several assertions about the existing codebase and environment that an executor would need to verify before relying on them:

1. **'solidlsp (already in repo)'**: The plan asserts solidlsp is already present in the rebar repository. If it is not, or if it is present but not wired as an importable library/subprocess, the polyglot tooling design breaks at the foundation. Cannot confirm without codebase access.

2. **'reusing rebar.llm extension points'** (criteria registry + orchestrator AC): The plan assumes `rebar.llm` already exposes catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points compatible with the criteria-registry pattern being proposed. If those extension points don't exist or require significant rework, this is a major undisclosed dependency.

3. **'extends the close-gate signing'** (attestation AC): The plan asserts the close-gate already has a signing mechanism that can be extended. The design of the new attestation depends on this. If the close-gate signing is structurally different or tightly coupled, the extension assumption may be wrong.

4. **'reducer-IGNORED SIDECAR'**: The plan asserts a `REVIEW_RESULT` event type can be made reducer-ignored cleanly, implying the event reducer has a documented skip/ignore mechanism. This needs verification against the reducer's actual architecture.

5. **'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time'**: This implies infrastructure for centrality computation already exists or is straightforward to add. No AC or design note addresses where this computation comes from.
findings.suggested_edit:
Add a brief 'Verified prerequisites' subsection to the plan that explicitly confirms (or flags as TBD) each of the following before implementation begins:
- solidlsp is importable and has a documented API surface in the repo
- rebar.llm exposes the specific extension points (catalog, prompt runner, etc.) assumed by the criteria registry
- The close-gate signing mechanism is extensible in the way described
- The event reducer has a documented ignore/skip mechanism for event types
- Centrality/blast-radius computation infrastructure exists or is being added in this epic

For any item that cannot be confirmed pre-implementation, add a discovery/spike sub-task to the child decomposition.

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' and 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented: … intent fidelity (2-step blind-restate) …'
findings.finding: Two acceptance criteria depend on artifacts or processes that may not be completable within a single working session. First, 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' requires that experiment work already be complete and its results available; if the linked experiments haven't been run yet, this criterion cannot be verified in-session. Second, the '2-step blind-restate' intent-fidelity check is described at a high level but without specifying what inputs/outputs constitute a passing test, making in-session verifiability unclear. The rest of the criteria (signing, sidecar events, fail-open tooling, etc.) appear mechanically verifiable in-session.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) confirm that the experiments are a prerequisite that will be completed before this epic begins (and note that in the plan), or (b) add an AC that specifies interim defaults with a 'to be tuned' note, so the agent can make measurable progress without blocking on external results. For the 2-step blind-restate check, add a concrete definition of what a passing restate looks like (e.g., 'restated plan covers ≥ N key elements of the original').

findings.location: Context section; Design summary
AC: 'Docs: how the gate works, the config surface, fail-open behavior, the cert semantics, and the CI-check pattern' vs. Success Criteria and Design summary
findings.finding: The plan is thorough and most ACs map cleanly to described design elements. One potential orphan: the AC item 'Docs: how the gate works…' has no corresponding description section that scopes what documentation is required (format, location, depth). Additionally, the 'Follow-on work' section lists F11 ('rigor-signature chain / CI integration helpers') as a separate ticket, yet the docs AC includes 'the CI-check pattern' — it is mildly ambiguous whether CI integration helpers are in or out of this epic. The term 'centrality/blast-radius' is used in the design summary and ACs but never defined, which could cause inconsistent implementation. These are minor coherence gaps rather than outright orphans.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief docs scope note (e.g., 'a README section in the rebar docs directory covering…') so the agent knows exactly where and how much documentation to write. Clarify whether the 'CI-check pattern' doc is an explanation of how to use the existing cert (in-scope) vs. CI tooling/helpers (F11, out-of-scope). Define 'centrality/blast-radius' once in the design summary so it is used consistently.

findings.location: Multiple locations: 'chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB … default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; 'escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality (centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time)'; 'Generous initial budget + depth posture … per-plan budget cap'
findings.finding:
Several design decisions are deferred to empirical results or left without concrete starting values, which an executing agent would need to resolve before coding:
1. Chunking defaults: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' — the agent cannot ship a default without a concrete number or a fallback heuristic.
2. 'Per-plan budget cap' — no unit (tokens? dollars? seconds?) or magnitude is given.
3. 'Escalation by size + complexity + centrality' — the thresholds for escalation are unspecified.
4. 'Generous initial budget + depth posture' — 'generous' is qualitative, not actionable.
5. 'A project can configure … per-criterion blocking via config, with sensible defaults shipped' — the config schema/format is not described anywhere in the plan.
An agent implementing this would face real decision points at each of these, risking inconsistent or arbitrary choices.
findings.suggested_edit: For each deferred decision, either (a) provide a concrete placeholder value the agent should use (e.g., 'default chunk size = 3 criteria per LLM call, to be tuned later'), or (b) explicitly add an AC requiring the agent to select and document a specific value with rationale. Describe the config schema at a high level (e.g., 'YAML under rebar.config.plan_review with fields: chunk_size, budget_cap_usd, blocking_criteria[]'). For the budget cap, specify the unit and a starting value.

findings.location: Title; Context; Design summary; ACs
Acceptance Criteria section (all 12 bullet points); Success Criteria section
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan whatsoever. None of the acceptance criteria, success criteria, or design sections describe: (a) how the gate's own behavior will be tested (e.g., unit tests for Layer-1 deterministic checks, integration tests for the signing/invalidation flow, tests for fail-open behavior on unsupported languages), (b) failure-path or boundary cases (e.g., what happens when the LLM is unavailable mid-review, when the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar write fails, when `--force` is used), (c) RED-before-GREEN ordering, or (d) any test harness or fixture strategy. For a gate that is supposed to block agent progress, the absence of tests for the gate itself is a significant gap — particularly for the polyglot fail-open guarantee, which is the kind of subtle regression that requires explicit testing.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a dedicated 'Testing' section (or an AC bullet) that covers at minimum: (1) unit tests for each Layer-1 check, including a test that exercises fail-open on an unsupported/crashing tool; (2) an integration test for the full open→in_progress gate flow (valid cert passes, missing cert blocks, material-edit invalidation blocks); (3) a test for the `--force` override path logging a justification; (4) tests for the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event schema and that the reducer ignores it. Specify whether tests run in the existing rebar test suite (pytest?) and whether a mock LLM is used for Layer-2.

findings.location: Success Criteria + AC bullet: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: The plan delegates the empirical grounding for key defaults (chunking, budget, depth) to a linked experiment work item that is not described in this plan. The 'done' state for those defaults is therefore unverifiable from the plan text alone. Additionally, the AC lists 12 acceptance-criteria bullets covering many sub-systems (Layer-1, Layer-2, attestation, REVIEW_RESULT, claim integration, docs, etc.), but there is no ordered sequencing or integration-test strategy that shows how all of them are verified together as a coherent gate. It is plausible the experiment work is tracked separately and well-defined, but that cannot be confirmed from this plan.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a sentence or AC bullet explicitly stating what 'experiment results' must produce (e.g., 'Experiment ticket EXP-X delivers empirically chosen chunk-size and budget defaults; this epic's defaults are set from those results before merge'). Consider adding an integration-test AC: 'An end-to-end test exercises open→in_progress with a known-flawed plan and verifies the gate blocks/coaches correctly.'

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all 12 bullets) + 'Follow-on work' section (F1–F12)
findings.finding: This is described as an 'epic' but has no child-ticket decomposition within the plan itself. The 12 AC bullets span at least 7–8 distinct implementation domains: (1) Layer-1 deterministic criteria + polyglot tooling, (2) Layer-2 advisory criteria, (3) criteria registry + orchestrator, (4) attestation + signing, (5) claim integration, (6) routing/chunking/budget/escalation, (7) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer change, (8) docs. Each of these is likely a multi-session effort on its own. Without a decomposition into child tickets, an agent assigned this epic would either attempt to one-shot an enormous scope or make ad-hoc sub-task decisions not aligned with the plan. The Follow-on work section lists F1–F12 as future tickets, correctly, but the core epic work is not similarly decomposed.
findings.suggested_edit: File child tickets (or stub them within this plan) for each major implementation domain (Layer-1 tooling, Layer-2 criteria, orchestrator, attestation, claim integration, routing/budget, sidecar event, docs/experiments). List them in the epic's AC or as a 'children' section with dependency ordering. The epic itself should then be a container whose 'done' is the union of its children.

findings.location: Design summary: 'solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep (resolution/callers); syft + registry + LLM-fallback (deps); scc + lizard (size)' and 'reusing rebar.llm extension points' and 'extends the close-gate signing'
findings.finding: The plan makes several load-bearing assumptions about the current state of the codebase that cannot be confirmed from the plan text: (1) solidlsp is 'already in repo' and usable for the resolution use-cases described; (2) 'rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' extension points exist and are sufficient for the criteria registry/orchestrator pattern described; (3) the close-gate signing mechanism exists in a form that can be 'extended' without significant rework; (4) the 'completion-verifier' architecture is close enough in shape to be the inverse pattern assumed. If any of these assumptions are wrong, large portions of the design collapse. These are plausible given an active codebase but must be verified before execution.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Verified preconditions' section listing: solidlsp integration point and its API contract, rebar.llm extension points (catalog/runner), close-gate signing interface, and completion-verifier shape. An executor should confirm each before designing the new gate.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Trigger / enforcement: fires at open→in_progress. claim is a FAST check' and AC bullet: 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'
findings.finding: The plan names the 'claim' command/path as the integration point for the fast attestation check, but no other file, module, or symbol is named. For a gate that touches the open→in_progress transition, there is likely a state-machine handler, an event reducer, and a CLI command that all need updating. The 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar' also implies a reducer change (adding a skip rule for the new event type). Without naming these targets, the blast radius of the change is unacknowledged at the file level. This may be intentional for an epic (vs. a leaf ticket), but it limits an executor's ability to scope the work.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files/modules expected to change' subsection (even at a coarse level): e.g., 'claim command module, open→in_progress state-machine handler, event reducer (add REVIEW_RESULT skip), attestation/signing module (extend from close-gate), new plan_review/ package.' This makes blast radius concrete without over-specifying implementation.

findings.location: Design summary overall

Design summary ('Routing pivots on has_children + complexity, NOT ticket type') vs. AC bullet ('Bugs are exempt from this gate (routed to a follow-on)') vs. Design summary ('Bugs are SKIPPED in this epic')
findings.finding: There is a mild tension between two routing axes: (1) the design says routing pivots on 'has_children + complexity, NOT ticket type', but (2) the bug exemption IS a type-based routing decision. This is not a true contradiction — bugs being exempt is an upstream filter before the complexity router — but it is not explicitly reconciled in the plan. An executor could misread the 'NOT ticket type' principle as meaning all ticket types, including bugs, go through the gate. A second minor tension: the 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in experiment results' AC bullet implies the experiments must complete before this epic can be called done, but there is no explicit dependency or sequencing stated between the experiment work and this epic.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify the routing description: 'Bugs are filtered out before the complexity/has_children router; among non-bug tickets, routing pivots on has_children + complexity, NOT ticket type.' For the experiment dependency, add: 'This epic is dependent on EXP-X delivering empirical defaults; the docs/config AC is not closeable until those results are in.'

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' and AC: 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented: measurability/agent-completable, ... anti-slop; plus triggered overlays (router-selected) and a bounded broad pass.'
findings.finding: Two ACs are unverifiable in-session without external resources. (1) 'Defaults grounded in experiment results (see linked experiment work)' defers measurability to an external experiment whose outcomes are not stated in the plan — an agent cannot confirm this criterion is met without inspecting the linked experiment, which may not be accessible. (2) 'triggered overlays (router-selected)' and 'bounded broad pass' are described in the design summary but no concrete triggering conditions, overlay identifiers, or scope bounds are written down in the ACs, making it unclear how an agent would verify the criterion is complete. Several other ACs are large but internally self-contained (e.g., all Layer-1 checks listed explicitly), so they pass. The AC 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec (grounded/specific/ranked/affirming/checklist)' is measurable by inspection of outputs. The experiment-dependency AC is the sharpest gap.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline the key experiment conclusions (e.g., 'default chunk size = N criteria per LLM call; per-plan token budget cap = X') so the criterion is self-contained, or reframe the AC as 'Configurable defaults are documented and a justification comment references the experiment session ID' — making it verifiable without running the experiments again.

findings.location: Context section: 'rebar has a completion-verifier close gate… It has no equivalent at the start of work… Flawed plans… get executed anyway, and early errors compound over autonomous agent trajectories (~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects).'

AC: 'Docs: how the gate works, the config surface, fail-open behavior, the cert semantics, and the CI-check pattern.' vs Success Criteria: 'The signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process as a rigor/anti-vibe-coding signal.'
findings.finding: Two potential mismatches worth flagging: (1) The Success Criteria item 'The signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process' is listed as a standalone success criterion, but the corresponding AC only mentions 'docs' for the CI-check pattern — no AC explicitly requires a machine-readable attestation format or a CI-check command/script. This creates a gap between the stated goal and what is verifiably built. (2) The design summary mentions 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' in two different places (Execution and Attestation sections) with slightly different framing — one as a routing input, one as a coverage record artifact — but neither the Success Criteria nor the ACs have a dedicated criterion for the blast-radius computation artifact, making it described-but-uncovered work. These are minor rather than major gaps because both items are plausibly subsumed by broader ACs, but they are ambiguous enough to cause executor confusion.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a specific AC line: 'The attestation output includes a machine-readable format (e.g., JSON/YAML) sufficient for an external CI step to verify the signature and pass/fail the build.' This closes the gap between the success criterion and the implementation criterion. For blast-radius: either add it explicitly to the attestation AC or note in the design it is an internal implementation detail with no separate deliverable.

findings.location: Design summary: 'chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB… default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' / AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).' Also: 'AMBIGUOUS escalates to a higher-tier adjudicator' (Design summary, Verdict model).
findings.finding: Several design decisions are explicitly deferred to external artifacts or left as TBDs that an executing agent would have to resolve: (1) The chunk-size and budget-cap defaults are deferred to 'experiment results' in the linked session log — the plan does not state the values, so the agent must either re-run experiments (out-of-session) or arbitrarily choose values. (2) 'AMBIGUOUS escalates to a higher-tier adjudicator' — no adjudicator is named, no interface defined, no fallback if unavailable. The agent has no concrete target to implement against. (3) 'triggered overlays (router-selected)' — no list of trigger conditions or overlay identifiers is given; the agent must invent them. (4) 'a bounded broad open-ended pass' — 'bounded' is not quantified (token budget? criterion count?). These are not minor ambiguities; they represent design decisions the agent will have to make, which undermines the plan's stated purpose of being executable without clarification.
findings.suggested_edit: For each deferred decision, either: (a) inline the resolved value (e.g., 'default chunk = 3 criteria; per-plan budget cap = 50k tokens'), (b) constrain the decision space ('choose from [X, Y, Z]'), or (c) explicitly list it as a design sub-task with a defined deliverable and acceptance signal. For the 'higher-tier adjudicator': name the interface (e.g., 'escalate to the same model tier used by the completion-verifier') or define a placeholder that the agent should leave configurable.

findings.location: Title: 'Plan-review verification gate (open→in_progress)'; Design summary; Success Criteria; AC list.
findings.finding: The plan body faithfully serves its stated title throughout. The design summary, success criteria, and ACs all focus on the open→in_progress gate. The explicit carve-outs (bugs exempted, follow-on items F1–F12 explicitly out of scope) show disciplined scope control rather than drift. No contradictions found between headline intent and detail.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (full list) and Success Criteria (full list) — no AC or success criterion mentions testing approach.
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan whatsoever. There is no mention of unit tests, integration tests, behavioral tests, or even structural assertions for any of the new components: the Layer-1 deterministic checks, the Layer-2 advisory criteria orchestrator, the attestation signing/invalidation logic, the `claim` integration, the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event, or the criteria registry. For a feature of this complexity — especially one that itself enforces plan quality for other agents — the absence of a testing strategy is a significant gap. There is no RED-before-GREEN discussion, no failure-path coverage (e.g., what happens if the LSP server crashes mid-review, or if attestation signing fails), and no mention of how the fail-open behavior of polyglot tooling will be tested (e.g., asserting that an unsupported language returns PASS rather than FAIL). The completion-verifier is mentioned as a precedent but its testing pattern is not referenced as a model.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC or a dedicated 'Testing approach' subsection covering: (1) unit tests for each Layer-1 check (including fail-open / unsupported-language paths); (2) integration tests for the claim→attestation check flow with a mocked review; (3) a test that a material edit invalidates the attestation; (4) a test that `--force` override is logged in the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar; (5) behavioral tests for the Layer-2 orchestrator verifying per-criterion verdict structure. Reference the completion-verifier test suite as a structural pattern to follow.

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: This AC defers to external experiment results that are not described in the plan itself. An agent cannot verify this criterion without access to the linked session log ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b') and the experiment outputs. Similarly, AC 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings … that measurably help an agent revise the plan' introduces a behavioral outcome ('measurably help') that is not tied to a concrete, in-session measurable signal (e.g., pass/fail rate on a fixture, delta in finding counts). It's unclear how an agent would verify 'measurably help' within a single session without real-user or multi-session data. The CI-check AC ('checkable by an external CI process') is verifiable by contract/schema but could also be read as requiring a live CI environment.
findings.suggested_edit: Replace 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' with a concrete proxy, e.g., 'Layer-2 findings each include a criterion reference, a grounded location quote, a severity rating, and a suggested edit field, verified by unit tests on fixture tickets.' For the experiment-defaults AC, inline a summary table of the experiment conclusions (chunk size, budget cap, depth thresholds) directly in the plan so the executor doesn't need the session log.

findings.location: Context section and Design summary
AC list vs. Design summary — 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points'
findings.finding: The plan body mentions 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' and the AC echoes 'reusing rebar.llm extension points', but neither the description nor the AC defines what those extension points are or how to confirm reuse vs. reimplementation. Without codebase access this is unverifiable. Additionally, the AC 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec (grounded/specific/ranked/affirming/checklist)' maps to the coaching/feedback spec in the design summary, but the coaching spec itself is only partially described inline — the full spec is said to live in the session log. This creates an orphan criterion whose acceptance test depends on an external document. Minor terminology inconsistency: the design summary alternates between 'Layer-1' / 'LAYER 1' and uses both 'close gate' and 'completion-verifier' for the same concept.
findings.suggested_edit: Inline the minimum coaching spec (the five named properties: grounded/specific/ranked/affirming/checklist) with one concrete example in the plan body, so the AC is self-contained. Standardise casing to 'Layer-1'/'Layer-2' throughout.

findings.location: Multiple sections
findings.finding:
Several design decisions are explicitly deferred or underspecified in ways that would force an executing agent to make consequential architectural choices:
1. 'chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB … default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' — the actual default values are not stated in the plan; the agent must consult the external session log.
2. 'escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality' — no threshold values, model names, or escalation logic are given.
3. 'per-criterion config knob lets a project make a judgment criterion BLOCKING' — the config schema/format is not described (file name, key names, value types).
4. 'AMBIGUOUS escalates to a higher-tier adjudicator' — what the adjudicator is, how it is invoked, and what happens if it is unavailable are unspecified.
5. AC 'Triggered overlays (router-selected)' — the routing conditions and the overlay criteria themselves are not named.
6. 'extends the close-gate signing' — implies the executor must understand existing close-gate code; if the extension interface is not documented here, this is a hidden dependency.
These are real design decisions, not implementation details, and will require the agent to stop and guess or investigate.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Deferred decisions / open questions' section that either (a) resolves each item with a concrete default (e.g., 'default chunk size = 3 criteria per call', 'escalation model = gpt-4o', 'config key: plan_review.criteria.<id>.blocking = true|false') or (b) explicitly names them as executor discretion with a bounded range. Inline the experiment-derived defaults table from the session log.

findings.location: Title, Context, Design summary, Success Criteria
Acceptance Criteria — no testing-plan AC or section present
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan, no test AC, and no mention of how any of the implemented components will be tested. Specifically absent: (1) unit or integration tests for Layer-1 deterministic checks (e.g., fixture tickets with known structural flaws to assert FAIL, and fixture tickets with correct structure to assert PASS/fail-open); (2) tests for the attestation signing and invalidation-on-material-edit logic; (3) tests for the `claim` integration path (with and without a valid attestation); (4) tests for the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event emission and the reducer-skip behaviour; (5) any mention of red-before-green ordering. The closest thing is the implicit 'experiment results' reference for tuning defaults, which is calibration data, not a test plan.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Testing approach' section (or a dedicated AC) covering: fixture-based unit tests for each Layer-1 criterion (one passing, one failing, one unsupported-lang fail-open fixture per check); contract/schema tests for the attestation and REVIEW_RESULT event; an integration test for the claim gate (missing sig → blocked, valid sig → allowed, --force → override logged); and at minimum a smoke test for the Layer-2 advisory path returning well-formed coaching output on a known fixture ticket.

findings.location: Success Criteria + Acceptance Criteria sections; 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: Most acceptance criteria are binary and checkable (e.g., 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check', 'REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event', 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented'). However, two AC items reference external artifacts the plan does not itself define: (1) 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' — the experiment work is a prerequisite or sub-task that is not captured in this epic's scope or linked as a child, making it unclear when that specific AC is satisfied. (2) 'Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' — the plan-of-record is external and not reproduced; if that log is unavailable or inconsistent with this summary, executors cannot verify conformance. The end-state is otherwise well-scoped: a signed attestation, claim integration, sidecar event, and docs are each discrete and verifiable. The gap is specifically around the empirical-experiment prerequisite and the external session-log dependency.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) file the experiment work as an explicit dependency/child ticket and state 'AC is blocked until experiment ticket #X is closed', or (b) inline the default values directly into the AC so an executor can verify them without the session log. Also add a brief 'done means' summary sentence: e.g., 'Done when: a claim attempt on an unreviewed ticket is blocked, a reviewed ticket's claim succeeds, a REVIEW_RESULT event is persisted, and the docs page is merged.'

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all 12 bullet points); Design summary (all sub-sections); 'This epic builds a plan-review verification gate'
findings.finding: This is an explicitly self-described epic with 12 distinct acceptance criteria spanning: (1) Layer-1 deterministic tooling integration (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard) across multiple languages; (2) a separate Layer-2 LLM-based advisory review with ~13 named criteria; (3) a criteria registry + generic orchestrator (reusing rebar.llm); (4) an attestation/signing subsystem; (5) claim integration; (6) configurable chunking, routing, escalation, and budget-cap logic; (7) a coaching/feedback authoring spec; (8) a REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer modification; (9) per-criterion config knobs; (10) bug-exemption routing; (11) empirical experiment-derived defaults; (12) documentation. Each of items 1–12 is independently complex enough to be a leaf ticket. The plan lists 12 follow-on items but does NOT decompose this epic into child tickets — it explicitly defers children as 'to be filed'. An agent attempting to execute this as a single unit will face an unbounded session, cross-cutting decisions made inconsistently, and no clear atomic checkpoint. The plan itself acknowledges the risk of 'too big to one-shot' plans (it's the motivating problem statement), yet does not apply that standard to itself.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into at minimum the following child tickets before claiming: C1 Layer-1 deterministic checks (tool integration + fail-open); C2 Layer-2 advisory criteria + orchestrator; C3 criteria registry + rebar.llm reuse; C4 attestation + signing + invalidation; C5 claim integration (fast check + --force); C6 routing/chunking/budget/escalation config; C7 REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip; C8 per-criterion config knob; C9 experiment work for defaults; C10 docs. This epic ticket then becomes the parent coordinating those children.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep (resolution/callers); syft + registry + LLM-fallback (deps); scc + lizard (size)'; 'Criteria registry: … reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'; 'Attestation … extends the close-gate signing'
findings.finding: The plan makes several unverified structural assertions about the existing codebase that an executor must confirm before implementation: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' — assumes solidlsp is already integrated and usable; if it is only vendored but not wired, this changes scope. (2) 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' — assumes these extension points exist and are stable enough to be reused without modification. (3) 'extends the close-gate signing' — assumes the close-gate signing mechanism is factored in a way that can be extended (e.g., a shared utility vs. inline code). (4) 'ast-grep' is listed as available but no confirmation of version compatibility or installation is noted. These are not asserted to be wrong — they may all be correct — but an executor should verify each before treating them as free reuse.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Prerequisites / verified assumptions' section listing: solidlsp integration status (import path / entry point), rebar.llm extension-point stability (which module/class/function is the hook), close-gate signing reuse surface (function name + module), and ast-grep availability in the toolchain. Flag any that are unconfirmed as 'TBD — verify before implementation'.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Polyglot tooling'; Acceptance Criteria: 'file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep)', 'package existence (syft + registry + fallback)'; 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'
findings.finding: The plan does not name specific files, modules, or symbols it will create or modify. For an epic this is partially expected, but several edit targets are implied and their blast radius is unacknowledged: (1) The 'claim' command/handler is modified to add the fast attestation check — any callers of claim (CLI, API, agent harness) may need updates. (2) The rebar.llm orchestrator is extended — other features reusing it may be affected. (3) The reducer is modified to skip REVIEW_RESULT events — all reducer tests and the event schema need updating. (4) The close-gate signing is extended — risk of regression to the existing close gate. None of these blast-radius concerns are acknowledged. Because the live codebase is not available, the actual file names and callsite counts cannot be confirmed — marking AMBIGUOUS rather than FAIL.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Known blast-radius touchpoints' subsection naming: the claim handler module, the reducer event-type registry, the close-gate signing utility, and the rebar.llm orchestrator entry point. Explicitly note 'existing close-gate tests must remain green' as an AC or a risk note.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Execution: chunking … default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; Acceptance Criteria: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; Follow-on: 'F1 FP/remediation-rate tracking tooling'
findings.finding: Two potential cross-section tensions are present: (1) The AC requires defaults 'grounded in experiment results', but the experiment work is not scoped in this epic nor listed as a child — it is referenced only obliquely as 'linked experiment work'. If experiments are not yet run, the AC for defaults cannot be satisfied within this epic. The plan's Design summary implies experiments inform the defaults before shipping, but the Follow-on list does not contain them (suggesting they should precede the epic, but this is not stated). This is a sequencing ambiguity that could block AC satisfaction. (2) The Follow-on item F1 ('FP/remediation-rate tracking tooling') is deferred, yet the AC item 'REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event sufficient to reconstruct per-criterion FP/remediation analysis offline' is in-scope. The data capture is in-scope but the analysis tooling is follow-on — this is intentional and coherent, but the boundary should be explicit to avoid scope creep. These are not hard contradictions but are sequencing gaps that could cause confusion during execution.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Dependencies and sequencing' note: (a) experiment work must be completed (or stubbed with placeholder defaults) before this epic can close AC-11; (b) explicitly restate that REVIEW_RESULT is capture-only in this epic and F1 is the downstream consumer.

findings.location: Success Criteria + AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: The plan delegates a critical completion signal — the empirically derived defaults for chunking, budget, and depth — to a linked experiment whose results are not present in the plan text. The phrase 'see linked experiment work' appears in the final AC bullet but no experiment ticket or result summary is referenced. Without those numbers, it is impossible to verify that the 'experiment' AC is satisfiable within this epic's own boundary, or to know when the epic is truly done vs. waiting on an unresolved dependency. Additionally, the plan conflates the review gate firing mechanism (out-of-band heavy review + fast claim check) with the termination condition for the gate itself: it is clear when a CLAIM succeeds or fails, but the AC does not state when the out-of-band review process is considered 'complete' (e.g., all criteria returned non-INDETERMINATE, or budget exhausted, or checkpointing committed). The INDETERMINATE outcome path is mentioned as 'non-blocking by default' but its terminal state is not articulated in the ACs.
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Add a reference to the specific experiment ticket(s) or embed a summary table of empirical defaults directly in the plan so the AC can be verified without chase. 2. Add an explicit AC that defines the terminal state of an out-of-band review run: e.g., 'A review run is considered complete when all scheduled criteria chunks have returned a verdict or the per-plan budget cap is exhausted and an INDETERMINATE outcome is recorded.'

findings.location: AC list (12 bullet points); Design summary; Follow-on work (F1–F12)
findings.finding: This is a leaf-level epic ticket that bundles at minimum 12 distinct deliverable sub-systems: (1) Layer-1 deterministic criteria (8 sub-checks), (2) Layer-2 advisory criteria (11+ sub-checks + triggered overlays + broad pass), (3) criteria registry + generic orchestrator, (4) attestation signing + invalidation logic, (5) `claim` integration, (6) configurable chunking/routing/escalation + budget cap + checkpointing, (7) feedback-authoring enforcement (coaching spec), (8) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip, (9) per-criterion config knob, (10) bug-exemption routing, (11) experiment-grounded defaults, and (12) documentation. Each of these is individually non-trivial (some require LSP integration, syft, ast-grep, LLM orchestration, signing, event sourcing changes). The plan acknowledges this is an 'epic' but presents it as a single implementation unit with no child tickets and no decomposition plan. The follow-on list (F1–F12) covers only post-delivery concerns, not the delivery itself. An agent attempting to execute this in one session would almost certainly fail to reach a shippable state.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose the epic into child tickets along vertical slices, for example: (a) Layer-1 structural floor only (deterministic checks + fail-open polyglot tooling); (b) Layer-2 advisory review engine (criteria registry + orchestrator + coaching-spec feedback); (c) Attestation + claim integration; (d) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + reducer skip; (e) Configurable chunking/routing/escalation + budget cap; (f) Experiment runs + defaults; (g) Documentation. Each child should be claimable and completable in one agent session. This epic ticket then becomes the container with has_children=true and can be reviewed under the container-coverage criteria.

findings.location: Design summary: 'solidlsp (already in repo)'; 'reusing rebar.llm extension points'; 'extends the close-gate signing'
findings.finding: Three load-bearing premises about the existing codebase are asserted without verification pointers: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' — the plan assumes solidlsp is present, functional, and sufficiently complete to support LSP queries across the target languages; any gap would invalidate the Layer-1 polyglot tooling design. (2) 'reusing rebar.llm extension points' — the plan assumes the rebar.llm module already exposes catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points compatible with the criteria registry pattern; if those extension points don't exist or have a different shape, the orchestrator design is invalid. (3) 'extends the close-gate signing' — the plan assumes the completion-verifier's signing mechanism is factored in a way that supports extension; this is unverified. Additionally, the reference to 'session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' as the plan-of-record is an external dependency that is not embedded and may not be accessible to an executing agent.
findings.suggested_edit: For each premise, add a verification note: e.g., 'solidlsp is present at <path>; confirm it supports <language list> before Layer-1 implementation begins.' For rebar.llm, name the specific extension points (e.g., module name, class, interface) so an agent can confirm they exist before designing the orchestrator. For the session log reference, embed the essential design decisions inline rather than relying on external log accessibility.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Trigger / enforcement: fires at open→in_progress. `claim` is a FAST check'; AC: '`claim` integration: fast signed-attestation check'
findings.finding: The plan names `claim` as the integration point and references the close-gate signing and the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event, but does not enumerate the specific files, modules, or symbols that will be touched. For a feature that threads through the state-machine (open→in_progress transition), the event reducer, the signing subsystem, the LLM orchestration layer, and the CLI, the blast radius is non-trivial. Without codebase access it cannot be confirmed whether the named extension points (`claim`, close-gate signing, rebar.llm) are the correct and only touch-points, or whether other callers of the open→in_progress transition exist (e.g., bulk-claim paths, API endpoints, tests for the state machine) that would also need updating. The plan does not acknowledge this blast radius explicitly.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files/symbols touched' section listing the expected modules (e.g., `rebar/claim.py`, `rebar/events/reducer.py`, `rebar/signing.py`) and explicitly note: 'All callers of the open→in_progress transition should be audited to ensure the gate check is not bypassable.' If the state machine has a single enforcement point, name it; if not, name all entry points.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Criteria registry: each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a 'criteria registry' with declarative descriptors and a 'generic orchestrator' pattern. Without codebase access it cannot be confirmed whether rebar.llm's existing catalog/contracts/prompt/runner already provides this capability or something close enough to reuse directly. If the existing pattern already handles per-criterion descriptor dispatch, a new 'registry' abstraction may be redundant (NIH/over-abstraction). Conversely, if the extension is genuinely new, this is appropriate. The plan's own language ('reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner') suggests awareness of this risk, but it is not resolved. The per-criterion config knob (advisory | blocking-with-override) is a configurable surface that could proliferate if not carefully bounded — the plan does not describe how many config dimensions the knob introduces per criterion or how the config schema is validated.
findings.suggested_edit: Confirm (in a verification note) that the criteria registry is not duplicating an existing rebar.llm dispatch mechanism. If reuse is possible, state explicitly what new code is needed vs. what is pure configuration of the existing system. For the per-criterion config knob, describe the schema (e.g., a single enum field per criterion entry in a YAML block) to show the surface stays bounded.

findings.location: Multiple cross-section contradictions listed below
findings.finding:
Three internal contradictions were identified:

1. **Epic vs. leaf sizing contradiction.** The 'Trigger / enforcement' section and the 'Routing pivots on has_children + complexity' design both describe a system that distinguishes container tickets from leaf tickets and applies different review depth accordingly. Yet this ticket itself is labeled an epic (container) but has no children — it is treated as a leaf for execution purposes. The gate it defines would flag itself as needing decomposition under its own Layer-2 child-coverage criterion ('child coverage + consistency (containers)'). This is a coherence failure between the design intent and the ticket's own structure.

2. **Fail-open 'IRONCLAD' vs. blocking Layer-1.** The design summary states Layer-1 'BLOCKS' and that unsupported lang/tool → 'skipped = PASS, never FAIL.' The AC states Layer-1 'blocks only on sound, unambiguous checks and fails open.' These are consistent in intent but the word 'BLOCKS' in Layer-1 combined with 'IRONCLAD' fail-open creates an apparent tension that is not resolved: what happens when a Layer-1 check partially completes (some languages resolved, one unsupported)? The AC does not describe partial-pass behavior.

3. **Experiment-grounded defaults vs. in-scope delivery.** The AC bullet 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' implies the experiments must be completed as part of this epic. However, no experiment child ticket or experiment work is described in the plan body, the decomposition, or the follow-on list. This creates a gap: either the experiments are in-scope (but unplanned) or out-of-scope (but the AC depends on them), which is a contradiction between the AC and the plan's scope.
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Either add child tickets to this epic (resolving the has_children=false contradiction) or reframe it explicitly as a 'design + spec only' ticket with implementation in children. 2. Add an AC clarification: 'Layer-1 partial completion (some tools available, some not) is treated as partial coverage recorded on the attestation; only supported checks contribute to blocking verdicts.' 3. Either file the experiment work as an explicit in-scope child ticket or move the 'experiment-grounded defaults' AC to a follow-on and replace it with 'sensible initial defaults documented; to be refined post-experiment.'

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' and AC: 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented: … anti-slop; plus triggered overlays (router-selected) and a bounded broad pass.'
findings.finding: Several acceptance criteria depend on externally-gated pre-conditions that may not be completable in one session. Specifically: (1) 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' defers the actual values to a linked experiment, meaning this AC cannot be closed until that experiment is complete — it is not self-contained. (2) The AC referencing 'triggered overlays (router-selected)' is not defined well enough to know whether the routing logic and all overlay criteria are implementable without further design decisions. (3) 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' (SC #3) is a success criterion phrased as an outcome observable only in future use — it is not verifiable within the session. The rest of the criteria appear structurally measurable and in-session completable (implement, test, emit event, sign, check signature, etc.).
findings.suggested_edit: Replace 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' with a proxy that is in-session verifiable, e.g. 'Each Layer-2 finding includes a grounded location reference, a severity label, and a suggested edit (when confidence is high), conforming to the coaching-spec format (verified by unit/integration test).' For the experiment-dependency AC, either inline the empirical defaults as starting values (with a note they may be revised) or explicitly break it into a follow-on child ticket.

findings.location: Context section: 'when an agent claims a ticket (open→in_progress), nothing checks that the ticket's implementation plan is sound before the agent executes it… ~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects.'
AC list vs. Design summary / Success Criteria
findings.finding: Three potential coherence gaps: (1) The Design summary mentions 'AMBIGUOUS escalates to a higher-tier adjudicator' — a non-trivial routing/escalation mechanism — but no AC explicitly covers the adjudicator interface or its behaviour. The closest AC ('escalation by size+complexity+centrality') covers model escalation, not AMBIGUOUS-verdict adjudication. (2) The Design summary states 'A per-criterion config knob lets a project make a judgment criterion BLOCKING (= block-with-`--force`-override)'; the AC entry 'Per-criterion config: advisory (default) | blocking-with-override' covers this, but the `--force` override path is only mentioned in the `claim` AC — the two together cover it, but the link is implicit. (3) SC #3 ('coaching findings that measurably help an agent revise the plan') has no corresponding AC that specifies how 'measurably help' is verified — there is an AC on format compliance but none on outcome verification. No outright orphans or contradictions were found; the issues are coverage gaps rather than true mismatches.
findings.suggested_edit: Add or expand an AC to explicitly address: (a) what happens when a Layer-2 verdict is AMBIGUOUS (is there an adjudicator endpoint, does it fall through to advisory, or is it always non-blocking?); (b) a measurable proxy for SC #3 (see F1 suggestion). Consider adding a cross-reference note in the `claim` AC pointing to the `--force` override behaviour defined in the per-criterion config AC.

findings.location: Multiple locations: (1) Design summary 'chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB… default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; (2) AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; (3) Design summary 'escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality (centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time)'; (4) Design summary 'Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b'
findings.finding: Four decision-deferral/undercooked placeholders that force the executing agent to stop and resolve real design decisions: (1) Default chunking/budget/depth values are explicitly deferred to an experiment — the agent cannot implement sensible defaults without those results and would have to make them up or halt. (2) The 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' mechanism is mentioned but never specified (algorithm, data source, thresholds), leaving implementation open-ended. (3) 'Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' — the plan relies on an out-of-band reference the agent must access to understand the full design; if that log is unavailable or long, the agent is blocked. (4) 'triggered overlays (router-selected)' — which criteria constitute the triggered overlays and what the routing conditions are is not spelled out in the plan.
findings.suggested_edit: (1) Inline starter defaults for chunking/budget/depth (even if provisional, clearly labelled TBD-from-experiment), so the agent can proceed and update later. (2) Specify the centrality algorithm (e.g., 'count of cross-ticket file-impact overlaps + import-graph in-degree; thresholds: low/medium/high at N=0, 1-5, >5') or move it to a follow-on. (3) Either inline the essential design decisions from session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b, or annotate which sections of the log are load-bearing. (4) Enumerate the triggered overlays and their activation conditions in an appendix or sub-section.

findings.location: Title 'Plan-review verification gate (open→in_progress)'; Design summary; AC list; Follow-on list
findings.finding: The body faithfully implements the gate-at-open→in-progress concept stated in the title. Scope exclusions (bugs, FP-rate tooling, CI helpers, cross-ticket semantic detection) are clearly relegated to named follow-on items. No work described contradicts the headline intent, and no headline intent is left undescribed. The follow-on list is disciplined and does not bleed back into this epic's scope.

findings.location: Entire AC list and Design summary — no dedicated testing section or testing-related AC exists.
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan whatsoever. There is no mention of: (1) unit tests for Layer-1 deterministic checks (especially the polyglot/fail-open paths — the most critical behaviour to test); (2) integration tests for the claim-gate enforcement (attestation present/absent/stale scenarios); (3) tests for the signing and invalidation logic (material vs. non-material event boundary); (4) failure-path coverage for the fail-open polyglot tooling (LSP crash, syft timeout, unsupported language → PASS); (5) tests for the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event schema and that the reducer skips it; (6) RED-before-GREEN ordering for any of the above. The only adjacent signal is the coaching-spec conformance check implied by the Layer-2 AC, but even that is not framed as a test. For a gate that affects all ticket claims, the absence of a testing plan is a significant rework risk.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Testing plan' section (or AC) covering at minimum: (a) Unit tests for each Layer-1 criterion with a fixture-based polyglot corpus including an unsupported-language case (verifies fail-open); (b) Integration test for the full claim flow: no attestation → blocked, stale attestation → blocked, valid attestation → allowed, --force override → allowed + override event recorded; (c) Property test or parametrised unit test for signing/invalidation boundary (material vs. non-material edits); (d) Unit test that the reducer ignores REVIEW_RESULT event type without error; (e) For Layer-2, a golden-output test that a known-bad plan fixture produces findings matching the coaching-spec format. Tests should be written before or alongside the implementation (RED-GREEN).

findings.location: Success Criteria + Acceptance Criteria sections; 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: The plan defers the calibration of key operational parameters (chunking defaults, budget caps, depth posture) to 'the experiment results' in a linked session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`). The experiments themselves are described as follow-on work that must be completed first ('Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'). This creates a dependency loop: the epic's own AC requires experiment outputs that are not yet in-scope or completed. It is unclear from the plan text how the executor knows the experiments are done, where the outputs live, or whether the epic is blocked until they conclude. The termination condition for this epic — 'all ACs checked green' — is therefore partially unverifiable at plan-time. Additionally, the AC for 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented' is a long flat list but there is no articulated integration test or end-to-end scenario that confirms the full gate fires correctly at open→in_progress, making holistic done-ness hard to confirm.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit pre-condition or sequenced sub-task: 'Experiments X, Y, Z must be complete and their outputs committed to [location] before this AC can be closed.' Alternatively, provide interim defaults (with values) that will be used if experiments are not yet done, and note that the AC requires either experiment-derived values or these stated fallbacks. Also add at least one integration-level AC: 'A ticket in state open can be claimed only after a valid attestation is present; an end-to-end test demonstrates that a missing or stale attestation blocks the claim and a valid one allows it.'
